data.title: OpenAI loses to a thousand questions: a cash register can't grow out of the chat box.
data.description:
Author: Kaori

In March 2026, OpenAI officially announced the abandonment of Instant Checkout.

Just six months ago, this feature was touted by Silicon Valley media as a crucial function laying the foundation for the future of Agentic Commerce. Sam Altman even described it on multiple occasions as a very important money-printing machine for OpenAI's future. Now, it has died from almost zero real transaction conversion.

Interestingly, on the other side of the Pacific, Alibaba's AI shopping feature, Qianwen, has already been fully opened for testing. You can say "a cup of milk tea" in the chat box, and the delivery person will knock on your door half an hour later.

Both are AI, both are chatbots, but one is a castle in the air that fades away in disgrace, while the other is a powerful force in consumer spending. The same path, drastically different results—the difference isn't because the model isn't smart enough. When we talk about AI shopping, what are we really talking about? In the second half of 2025, this was the hottest sector. ChatGPT had 900 million weekly active users; even if only one ten-thousandth of them made a purchase during a conversation, the revenue would be staggering. But in the six months from the high-profile launch of instant checkout to its cancellation, many cruel stories unfolded. "You can't run a supermarket without owning shelves." Let's rewind to September 2025, the day OpenAI announced instant checkout. The entire retail industry was ecstatic. Shopify President Harley Finkelstein called it the new frontier of online retail. On the day of the announcement, Etsy's stock price jumped 16%. Partners quickly materialized, and Etsy allowed US sellers' products to appear directly on ChatGPT, even covering transaction fees to expedite service launch. Walmart listed approximately 200,000 items. PayPal planned to integrate its wallet with ChatGPT checkout as part of the partnership, and also pledged to increase its purchases of OpenAI's API and enterprise ChatGPT subscriptions. Stripe and OpenAI jointly developed the Agentic Commerce Protocol, attempting to establish an industry standard for AI-assisted transactions. But six months later, the bubble burst. OpenAI had promised to integrate with over 1 million Shopify merchants, but only a paltry 30 or so were actually online. The landing page Shopify created specifically for ChatGPT now quietly redirects back to its official website homepage. Even more concerning, OpenAI's internal data shows that while many users browse and compare prices in ChatGPT, almost no one actually places an order through the chat interface. According to Walmart's data, the conversion rate for those who return to the retailer's official website to checkout is three times higher than those who remain in ChatGPT. Forrester's research confirms this; among frequent users of AI question-answering engines, making purchases within the engine is the lowest adoption rate. Why doesn't buying things in ChatGPT work? Because OpenAI is attempting to function as an e-commerce platform without owning any commercial infrastructure. The most superficial reason is habit. People use ChatGPT like they use Google—to search for information and make comparisons. Once they've chosen, they go to a trusted place to swipe their card. Asking users to enter their credit card number in an interface meant for ChatGPT inherently carries a sense of insecurity. Users are willing to let AI help them choose skincare products, but when it comes to actually paying, the cold, impersonal dialog box doesn't provide the reassurance they crave. Moreover, even if users were willing to spend money, OpenAI couldn't handle it. As of February 2026, OpenAI hadn't even built a system for collecting and paying sales tax across US states. This is infrastructure that Amazon and eBay spent years building. Not to mention fraud detection, returns processing, and consumer protection compliance. Ensuring real-time accuracy of price, inventory, and shipping information for millions of products isn't just about writing a few lines of elegant code; it's a quagmire. Forrester analysts pointed out that instant checkout was plagued with errors during its launch, lacked support for multi-item carts, had no promotional codes, and even shipping information was opaque. The most awkward situation isn't for OpenAI itself, but for the partners it was brought in. PayPal not only invested engineering resources in integration but also pledged to increase its purchases of OpenAI's APIs and enterprise subscriptions. Now, the returns on the shopping side have evaporated, but the purchase commitments remain. According to sources, PayPal and OpenAI are evaluating how to continue their relationship. Etsy's situation isn't easy either. It previously subsidized transaction fees for merchants, and now it has to build its own ChatGPT app from scratch, without even agreeing on a fee structure. An Etsy spokesperson said it's unclear even whether OpenAI will charge for in-app transactions. Stripe is in a slightly better position because it already handles OpenAI's consumer subscription payments, a revenue stream that doesn't rely on shopping features. But most partners don't have this buffer. For a company that is fully shifting its focus to enterprise clients, this constantly changing cooperation model is a huge hidden danger. Alibaba achieved this, but it was both a gift and a constraint from its ecosystem. On January 15, 2026, while OpenAI was still struggling with instant checkout, something happened on the other side of the world. Alibaba held a press conference in Hangzhou, announcing that the Qianwen App was fully integrated with Taobao, Alipay, Taobao Flash Sale, Fliggy, and Gaode Maps. At the press conference, Wu Jia, president of Qianwen's C-end Business Group, said to his phone, "Order me 40 cups of Bawangchaji's 'Bo Ya Jue Xian' tea," and the rider delivered them half an hour later. If we compare OpenAI's instant checkout and Qianwen's AI shopping, Qianwen's success isn't due to whose big model is smarter, but rather who owns the entire chain from finding products to receiving packages. Taobao's hundreds of millions of SKUs, Alipay's native payment system, and the logistics networks of STO Express, YTO Express, ZTO Express, and Yunda Express are readily available. When you say in Qianwen, "I'm going hiking in Siguniang Mountain next week, what equipment do I need?", it can directly pull up a list. You just click on the card to place the order—it's incredibly smooth because there are no cross-company data authorization negotiations or profit-sharing disputes involved. This is why domestic companies like DeepSeek and Kimi can't do this. No matter how powerful the reasoning ability or how robust the long text, without a product shelf and payment, all they can do is throw a link at you. Alibaba has integrated the product shelf into the chat box, while OpenAI is trying to make the chat box pretend to be a product shelf. But is this really a perfect final form? Alibaba succeeded because it forcefully incorporated its extremely cumbersome commercial ecosystem into a large model. However, when Qianwen acts as both referee and player, can its recommendations remain objective? If I ask Qianwen which phone is good, will it prioritize recommending a product from Taobao due to commercial interests? When an AI loses its neutral search stance and becomes a super shopping guide for its own e-commerce platform, can it still be considered a general-purpose brain? Essentially, this uses a cumbersome old ecosystem to hijack new technological entry points. Qianwen's "success" is not only a gift from the ecosystem but also a shackle. $50 Billion and the Elephant in the Room OpenAI also realized it lacked this cumbersome ecosystem, but that wasn't the whole reason for its abandonment. The real elephant in the room is Amazon. At the end of February, Amazon announced a $50 billion investment in OpenAI, becoming the exclusive third-party cloud provider for its enterprise platform, Frontier. When your biggest benefactor is a giant that holds 40% of the US e-commerce market share and is aggressively promoting its AI-powered shopping assistant, continuing to add a checkout function within your app makes you seem incredibly inconsiderate. Moreover, this sum of money is itself a powder keg. Microsoft believes that AWS's hosting of the Frontier platform violates its exclusive cloud agreement with OpenAI and is considering legal action. OpenAI's lawyers are using technical terms like "stateful architecture" to circumvent the spirit of the contract. To survive in the competition among giants, OpenAI must make trade-offs. In mid-March, OpenAI CEO Fidji Simo announced a major strategic shift at an all-hands meeting: "We cannot miss this moment because of side projects." What prompted OpenAI's urgency was Anthropic's rapid rise in the enterprise market. Claude Code and Cowork products have made Anthropic the top choice for enterprise customers. Ramp credit card data shows that new enterprise customers choose Anthropic three times more often than OpenAI. OpenAI expanded too rapidly last year: Sora video generation, the Atlas browser, Jony Ive hardware devices, e-commerce features, advertising, and an adult mode. Now, they must narrow their focus to two core battlegrounds: coding tools and enterprise customers. After all, making money from enterprise customers is much more reliable than squeezing a few percentage points from transaction fees in chat windows. OpenAI expects enterprise customers to contribute half of its total revenue this year, up from the current approximately 40%. To this end, it plans to double its workforce from 4,500 to 8,000, with the new employees focusing on engineering, research, sales, and product development. In San Francisco, OpenAI signed a new lease, expanding its office space to over 1 million square feet. The real battleground for AI shopping isn't at the checkout counter. OpenAI's retreat doesn't mean AI shopping is dead; on the contrary, the upper half of the funnel has been completely reshaped. More than half of American consumers are already accustomed to letting AI make decisions for them. Instead of searching for floor scrubbers and scrolling through ten pages of ads, they directly ask, "Which one offers the best value?" Discovery, research, and comparison—these actions are all being moved forward, and the value of retailers' own channels is being eroded at an accelerated pace. However, the last mile of the transaction loop requires not a smarter model, but a more complete infrastructure. OpenAI has clearly stated that it will prioritize product search and discovery, and add advertising to ChatGPT. This is its way of monetizing at the discovery layer, which is much more realistic than building its own checkout. Finally, the most likely entity in the US to replicate Alibaba's path is not OpenAI, but Amazon. It possesses user profiles, product graphs, payment channels, and fulfillment infrastructure. OpenAI failed in its attempt to build its own e-commerce loop, then took money from the largest e-commerce platform, and may ultimately become a traffic portal for that platform. In China, Alibaba's full-stack advantage led Qianwen down a different path, but currently, only Alibaba has been able to succeed on this path. Qianwen's president, Wu Jia, made a crucial statement: "Comprehensive Agents are extremely competitive, while vertical Agents are increasingly proving to be a phase-specific product. In the future, there won't be many AI applications that can stand alone as entry points." In business terms, this means that those platforms that already possess a complete ecosystem will be able to create a closed-loop AI shopping experience, not AI companies that build everything from scratch. A chat window can't grow into a cash register, but if the cash register is already in your store, placing a chatty AI next to it would seem perfectly natural. This is the most important lesson for AI shopping in 2026.

data.title: Revolut: $6 billion in revenue, 70 million users, and large-scale banking services.
data.description:
Author: insights4vc, Translated by: Shaw & Jinse Finance

Few privately held fintech companies have combined scale, growth, and profitability as well as Revolut. With over 70 million customers, a continuously expanding balance sheet, and an increasingly diversified revenue structure, the company is increasingly resembling a global digital bank rather than a traditional innovative fintech company.

In this report, we will analyze Revolut's development history, the advantages and constraints of its existing model, and the role of digital assets in its overall strategic direction.

；

Revolut 2025 Key Metrics

Summary

  • Scale: Revolut currently serves over 70 million individual customers and approximately 80 The company has 10,000 corporate clients, representing year-on-year growth of 30% and 33% respectively in 2025. Revenue reached $6 billion in 2025 (a 46% year-on-year increase), with pre-tax profit of $2.3 billion (a 57% year-on-year increase). Total client funds balance grew by 66% to $67.5 billion, exceeding the growth rate of the number of clients. Business Diversification: All 11 core product lines generated annual revenue exceeding $135 million, with a relatively balanced structure of subscription fees, payment fees, foreign exchange, wealth management, and interest income. Notably, Revolut Business, the corporate banking business, contributed approximately 16% of total revenue. The credit portfolio grew by 120% to $2.9 billion in 2025, with interest income ($974 million) increasing by 23% year-on-year as the credit portfolio expanded. While transaction fees (bank cards, foreign exchange, etc.) still account for the largest share, these changes are driving Revolut towards a more stable interest-based revenue structure. Customer Activity: Product innovation and network effects continue to drive high user stickiness. In Europe, approximately one-fifth of the working-age population uses Revolut. 16 million new users were added in 2025 (total users reached 68.3 million in December 2025), with 63% of new individual customers coming from organic referrals. Paid subscriptions increased by 42% year-over-year, and RevPoints loyalty program users surged from 6.6 million to 17 million, demonstrating the effectiveness of cross-selling. Data indicates that Revolut is increasing the share of wallets per user, rather than simply opening one-time accounts. Strategic Advantages: Revolut's core competitiveness includes a comprehensive product portfolio (banking, payments, wealth management, and crypto assets) and an agile technology platform. The company has achieved economies of scale in key markets such as the UK and Europe, and has deeply engaged users through the launch of new services such as mortgages and mobile communications. Low-cost digital operations and 24/7 online customer service effectively control costs, significantly improving operating leverage—the pre-tax profit margin reached 38% in 2025, higher than 35% in 2024, supporting its continued expansion. Management has repeatedly emphasized that a "diversified, highly resilient business model" is the core driver of record-breaking performance. Key Risks: Rapid growth is accompanied by regulatory and enforcement risks. In 2025, EU regulators fined Revolut's EU branch €3.5 million for anti-money laundering compliance loopholes; UK regulators launched a review of its cross-border risk control system, leading to a delay in the issuance of a full UK banking license. The average account balance per customer remains low, with only about 45% of users using Revolut as their primary account. Deposit funds and user retention capabilities need improvement. Analysts acknowledge that transaction fees (foreign exchange, credit card spending) and crypto asset-related revenue, which rely on market conditions, are cyclical, and sustainable profitability depends on rapid user and product growth. The acquisition of new qualifications such as a US banking license and the large-scale expansion of the lending business have not yet been fully validated. Crypto Asset Business: Revolut's crypto strategy is an integral part of its overall business, but not its core. The company launched its cryptocurrency trading service as early as 2017 and subsequently built a regulated digital asset platform (which has obtained an EU CASP license). Currently, it offers custodial crypto trading services (the Revolut X platform supports over 250 tokens) and advanced features such as zero-fee order placement and API access. However, crypto trading is only one of the wealth investment product lines (alongside stocks, ETFs, and staking), and management did not highlight it separately in the 2025 results. Analysis suggests that the current crypto business contributes limited to total revenue, primarily serving as a tool for user activation and engagement. Recent participation in the UK Financial Conduct Authority's sandbox experiment (a pilot program for stablecoins in the British pound) may indicate future innovation directions. Overall, crypto assets are a strategic growth area for Revolut, strengthening its super-application positioning and building a regulated digital financial bridge. However, this also comes with volatility and regulatory risks, positioning it as a supplement to, rather than a replacement for, its core banking business.

Revolut is one of the fastest growing financial institutions (Source: Revolut 2025 Annual Report, a16z analysis)

Strategic Overview

  • Established / Headquarters: 2015

Founded / Headquarters: 2015

Revolut's Development History

Revolut started in 2015 as a multi-currency prepaid debit card for travelers and expatriates, providing real-time foreign exchange services at interbank rates, solving the common pain point of users being troubled by high exchange fees. The company experienced rapid growth in its early stages, reaching approximately 300,000 users by 2016. Starting in 2017, Revolut significantly added new features, such as peer-to-peer payments, budget management tools, and paid subscription packages (Premium/Metal Edition), to increase user activity and generate recurring revenue. Crucially, Revolut launched cryptocurrency trading functionality for users at the end of 2017, directly supporting Bitcoin, Litecoin, and Ethereum trading within the app. This was a pioneering move among fintech companies, thus initiating a diversified revenue stream.

Revolut 2015-2020, data source: Whitesight

A significant strategic milestone was obtaining a European banking license through a Lithuanian bank at the end of 2018—Revolut was one of the first fintech companies to achieve this. This enabled it to hold customer deposits within the EU and offer IBAN accounts, supporting its ambitions to expand beyond pure electronic money business.

With its licenses secured, Revolut accelerated its expansion into new markets (Asia, the US, and Latin America) and added various banking features. For example, the company launched Revolut Business for corporate accounts in 2017, partnered with DriveWealth around 2019 to launch stock trading services, and launched zero-commission ETFs and other digital wealth management products in the European Economic Area in 2022-2023. Through continuous iteration (usually in the form of mobile app updates), Revolut maintained strong organic growth; in fact, most of its growth during 2020-2021 came from word-of-mouth. Regulatory landscape has profoundly shaped its development trajectory. As a company originating in the UK, Revolut shifted its services to EU users after Brexit, relying on its Lithuanian license. The company obtained a restricted banking license in the UK in mid-2024, but only received full banking approval in the UK in March 2026, enabling it to conduct deposit account and lending business in its home market. Each new license enhances market trust: deposits in the EU are protected by a Lithuanian scheme, while approximately 13 million UK users now enjoy deposit insurance protection under the UK Financial Services Compensation Scheme (FSCS). Throughout its development, Revolut has consistently positioned product bundling as a core competitive advantage (i.e., a "multi-engine platform" model). The launch of a new feature often drives other businesses: for example, adding travel insurance to premium packages increases card usage; integrating cryptocurrency and stock trading within the app enhances user stickiness. By 2025, the company had 11 independent product lines, each generating over £100 million (approximately $135 million) in annual revenue, fully demonstrating the effectiveness of its feature-driven growth strategy. In its early stages, focusing on low-cost international payments and card usage scenarios allowed Revolut to identify a niche market and achieve rapid growth. The subsequent integration of fintech services such as investment, insurance, and roaming SIM cards transforms it into a "super app" with higher user stickiness, enabling cross-selling to existing users. EU and UK banking licenses translate the convenience of fintech into mainstream market-recognized trust and balance sheet management capabilities. In short, from foreign exchange cards to crypto wallets, and then to a licensed bank, each upgrade deepens user engagement on the platform and opens up new revenue engines. As of the end of 2025, Revolut has become a large-scale, profitable fintech bank. Key data for 2025 are as follows: Approximately 68.3 million individual users (up 30% year-over-year), and 767,000 enterprise clients (up 33% year-over-year); Revenue reached $6 billion (up 46% year-over-year), and pre-tax profit reached $2.3 billion (up 57% year-over-year); Profit margins were strong, with a pre-tax profit margin of 38%, higher than the previous year's 35%, demonstrating economies of scale. These figures signify that the company has achieved net profit for five consecutive years, a stark contrast to many still unprofitable private fintech companies, and also indicate that Revolut's business model is gradually acquiring the characteristics of a large-scale financial institution.

User Engagement and Product Adoption

Growth remains strong. Revolut added 16 million individual customers in 2025. More importantly, user engagement continued to deepen. The number of customers choosing Revolut as their primary bank grew by approximately 45% annually, and the amount of data used per user continued to increase: total transaction volume increased significantly by 65% ​​in 2025, reaching $1.7 trillion, and the number of transactions per user increased by 24% year-over-year.

Nearly one-third of new accounts are Revolut accounts.

Revenue Structure

The company's business is highly diversified. Total revenue in 2025 is approximately £4.5 billion, of which about half comes from transaction fees (bank card fees, foreign exchange) and subscription fees, and the remainder from investment, interest, and other financial services income.

For example, subscription revenue was approximately £708 million, bank card fees were approximately £1 billion, and wealth management and foreign exchange combined totaled approximately £1.3 billion. The corporate segment (corporate accounts) contributed approximately 16% of revenue, while credit and interest income (personal loans and credit cards) grew rapidly: credit balances reached $2.9 billion (a year-on-year increase of 120%), and interest income reached $1.3 billion. In short, Revolut's current revenue is still primarily from fees, but it is gradually shifting towards interest income to create a complementary structure.

Revenue Mix

Operating Leverage

Economies of scale are evident, and cost efficiency continues to improve. A profit margin of 38% in 2025 indicates that the growth rate of new revenue significantly exceeds the growth rate of fixed costs. Management emphasizes implementing strict cost management (e.g., allocating 90% of assets to cash and government bond investments) and adhering to technology-driven operations. Efficiency improvements are clearly evidenced: despite continued user growth, customer support response times were reduced by 40% by 2025. However, continued expansion (new markets, new licenses, and a planned £10 billion capital expenditure over five years) means overall costs will still rise. The Balance Between Maturity and Growth: Revolut currently exhibits both growth potential and scalability. The company is no longer burning cash, and profits and strong metrics demonstrate its status as a scalable financial institution. At the same time, it continues to grow rapidly (user growth exceeding 20%, with even higher growth in emerging markets) and invests heavily in new products and new regional markets. We believe it remains a growth platform but has begun to exhibit the robust characteristics of a bank. The core question is whether the company can effectively leverage its scale advantages—for example, converting users into primary account holders and advancing balance sheet products such as credit and mortgages—to maintain both growth and profit margins. Business Model and Profitability Quality Revolut's business model is based on a combination of fee/commission income, subscription fees, and interest income, with the structure becoming increasingly balanced over time. In 2025, approximately 80% of revenue was non-interest income (bank card fees, foreign exchange spreads, trading commissions, subscription fees, and wealth management service fees), and approximately 20% was net interest income (primarily from personal lending). Core Revenue Sources: Trading and Subscription Fees: Bank card payment fees (interbank clearing fees/merchant fee sharing) and foreign exchange fees remain substantial (approximately $1 billion and $800 million respectively in 2025). Paid subscription packages (Plus/Premium/Metal) are another stable source of revenue (US$708 million in 2025, a 67% year-over-year increase). This type of revenue grows in tandem with the number of customers and the scale of spending, and while it is partly cyclical (linked to consumption and travel), it also has recurring characteristics. Wealth Management Service Fees: Investment services (stock trading, ETFs, CFD spreads) generated US$876 million in revenue in 2025, a 31% year-over-year increase. Although it represents a small percentage of total revenue, it reflects an important diversification strategy—Revolut earns commissions from stock/ETF trading and cryptocurrency trading. The zero-commission ETFs launched in 2025 are expected to attract more users, but currently, wealth management product revenue still accounts for less than 20%. Interest Income (Credit Business): Revolut's credit business is still in its early stages but is growing rapidly. As of the end of 2025, the loan balance reached $2.9 billion (mainly unsecured personal loans), driving interest income of $1.3 billion, a year-on-year increase of 23%. This is the first time Revolut has achieved annual net interest income close to $1 billion, accounting for approximately 20% of total revenue. As the company transforms into a bank (mortgage loans have been launched in Lithuania, and credit products are about to be launched in the UK), interest income is expected to eventually become one of its top two revenue sources. Importantly, interest income is relatively stable, allowing the company's profits to be linked to net interest margins rather than relying on volatile fees. Corporate and SME Segment: Corporate accounts, payments, and fintech services contribute approximately 16% of revenue. This business line has lower risk (serving large enterprises with high B2B transaction volume), stronger user stickiness (stable account relationships), and has significantly increased business scale—Revolut's enterprise business processed $365 billion in transactions in 2025. Continued growth in this segment (an average of 30,000 new enterprises per month in 2025) will optimize customer diversity and fee revenue stability. Profitability Quality: This business model has shown strong profitability after scaling, but is still susceptible to cyclical fluctuations. The organic growth of high-margin revenue (bank cards, foreign exchange) and the rapid expansion of credit business have resulted in double-digit net profit margins to date. However, caution is still warranted: fee revenue is highly correlated with consumption and travel (which are cyclical), and it also made a significant contribution to profits during the cryptocurrency trading boom in 2024; as the cryptocurrency market stabilizes, past performance is partly attributable to cyclical factors. On the other hand, the ever-expanding credit ledger provides more stable revenue, while the recurring nature of subscription fees creates a "flywheel effect" as users add more services. More than 11 revenue streams demonstrate resilience: a decline in a single business line (such as reduced travel) can be partially offset by other businesses (such as credit card spending or loans). Overall, Revolut's profit structure has matured from a single fintech model to a diversified, bank-like revenue mix—still growth-oriented, but more stable than in its early stages. Revolut's core competitive advantage lies in its rich feature matrix and rapid innovation iteration capabilities. It offers a comprehensive product system within a single application—bank accounts, multi-currency wallets, real-time foreign exchange transfers, global debit cards, loans, cryptocurrency and stock trading, insurance, mobile eSIM services, and more. Localization further enhances its competitiveness: for example, Revolut offers local IBAN accounts in several EU countries, launched mortgage services in Lithuania, and even operates a mobile virtual network operator (Revolut Mobile) in the UK and Poland. This localization capability makes it more tailored to local needs than general-purpose applications. Its user interface and experience (UX/UI) are often cited by research and media as key differentiators – the extremely simple account opening process, budget management tools, real-time notifications, and peer-to-peer payment capabilities are highly attractive. Revolut's technology platform also heavily utilizes artificial intelligence and automation, such as deploying advanced AI throughout the system to improve fraud detection capabilities, achieving up to 10 times the daily fraud checks of traditional methods. This reduces human intervention internally, helping to maintain security levels during large-scale expansion. Data analytics capabilities and open APIs (such as the Revolut X API for professional crypto traders) further enhance added value. Platform Model Revolut describes itself as a "multi-engine" platform, with its advantages partly stemming from cross-selling within the platform. For example, the RevPoints points system integrates travel and payment scenarios for card spending, incentivizing continued user engagement. The 17 million RevPoints users have also boosted activity across the entire product line. Similarly, integrating cryptocurrency and stock trading within a single bank account interface significantly reduces customer acquisition costs compared to independent brokerages. A large user base (68 million) creates a distribution advantage: Revolut data shows that 63% of new users come from referrals, forming a virtuous cycle of growth. Cost Structure As a purely digital institution, Revolut's fixed operating costs are far lower than traditional banks (without physical branches), and it can iterate features more quickly. This helps to strictly control costs, supporting a high pre-tax profit margin of 38%. However, compliance and cybersecurity costs continue to rise, and the company is also investing heavily in technology and talent recruitment (reaching 7,900 employees by mid-2025). If trading volume slows, its aggressive pricing strategy (such as Revolut X's zero-fee order placement) could put pressure on the unit economics of its crypto and trading businesses. Competitive Landscape Compared to traditional banks like Barclays and Santander, Revolut's advantages lie in its agility and product breadth. The launch of its UK current account service in March 2026 makes it a direct challenger to HSBC and Barclays. Facing fintech peers like Monzo, N26, and Wise, its core advantage is its global scale and multi-business line layout: N26 and Monzo focus on a single market, while Revolut's 70 million users are spread across dozens of countries. Compared to pure crypto exchanges like Coinbase and Binance, Revolut is licensed and compliant, and deeply integrated with comprehensive financial services, making it more attractive to users who want to manage all their assets with a single application. In short, Revolut's competitive positioning is built on its channel distribution capabilities and product breadth. While it may not have a unique technological moat besides its large user community, its seamless user experience, continuous innovation, and global regulatory footprint make it difficult for any single competitor to fully match. (It should be noted that "super app" is more of a marketing term, but Revolut's product portfolio is indeed far broader than that of ordinary digital banks.) Risks, Constraints, and Unresolved Issues The rapid growth in regulatory scrutiny has attracted strict regulatory scrutiny. In April 2025, the Lithuanian bank responsible for overseeing Revolut's EU banking operations fined Revolut €3.5 million for deficiencies in its anti-money laundering system. The penalty specifically addressed "violations and deficiencies in monitoring business relationships." Similarly, UK regulators (FCA/PRA) questioned Revolut's cross-border risk management capabilities, delaying the issuance of its full UK banking license. These events demonstrate that weak compliance or mismanagement can lead to further fines or business restrictions. Unresolved issues: Can Revolut's corporate governance and anti-money laundering system keep pace with its business expansion? License Approval and Operational Implementation Risks: Obtaining each new banking license takes several years. Although it has now secured a UK license, migrating users to the new banking system will still take months. Revolut is also awaiting approval for its US banking license and is expanding in Latin America (Mexico, Peru) and Europe (applying for a French license). Delays or unforeseen conditions similar to those in 2024 could hinder growth. Operationally, adapting to new regulatory requirements (such as establishing branches and meeting local capital requirements) could weaken its speed advantage. Credit and Funding Risks: As its lending business expands, Revolut faces unforeseen credit risks. The rapidly growing $2.2 billion in unsecured loans could impact profitability if a future economic downturn leads to increased default rates. Currently, Revolut's balance sheet is conservative (90% cash/government bonds), but risks remain as mortgage and consumer credit expand. Funding sources are also crucial: most Revolut user accounts still have low balances (analysts point out that its user deposits are lower than traditional banks). To support lending with internal funding, Revolut needs to increase user engagement to attract more deposits or seek external financing channels. Profit Margin Sustainability: The current high transaction volume and rapidly growing interest income have boosted profit margins, but this pattern may come under pressure. Transaction volume is partly influenced by consumer spending trends; if travel decreases or the economy slows, transaction revenue may decline. Competition from traditional banks and other fintech companies is also intensifying (e.g., banks launching their own multi-currency cards). Furthermore, some of Revolut's transaction revenue—especially from cryptocurrency trading—is naturally cyclical (bull markets see significant revenue growth). A sustained bear market in crypto assets or stocks will directly impact transaction fee revenue. Management plans to rely more on lending (a more stable revenue stream) to mitigate the impact, but this model has not yet been validated at Revolut's current scale. Geographic and Market Complexity: Expanding into diverse markets such as Mexico and the Asia-Pacific region requires navigating local competition and subtle regulatory differences. The UK and EU are highly homogenized markets with high technology penetration, while consumer behavior varies significantly in other regions. Revolut's relatively uniform application model may face challenges at the cultural or banking system level (such as insurance regulations and digital identity requirements in some countries). Balancing product standardization with localization is a major constraint. Managing 70 million users across nearly 100 jurisdictions is extremely complex. Expanding customer service is a long-term issue; although customer service response time is expected to decrease by 40% by 2025, future service failures (such as prolonged system outages, fraud incidents, or unjustified account freezes in the past) could damage user trust. Maintaining a robust technology and security system is crucial. Simultaneously, venturing into crypto assets and innovative products will attract new regulatory rules (EU MiCA, UK FCA, US SEC rules). Changes in regulatory policies (such as UK stablecoin regulation and strengthened consumer protection) may force product restructuring. Product Complexity and Business Focus A rich product portfolio is an advantage, but it also carries the risk of scattered resources. Managing numerous business lines, including banking, wealth management, crypto, communications, and loyalty programs, increases complexity and disperses resources. If new businesses fail to scale, it could drag down the core banking business. The company seems to recognize this risk and has proposed incubating "new business growth points" within its diversified platform, but control at the execution level will be crucial. Digital Asset Strategy and Prospects The cryptocurrency business was launched in 2017 as a convenient one-click entry point. Revolut launched its cryptocurrency trading service at the end of 2017, allowing users to buy and sell Bitcoin, Ethereum, Litecoin, and other tokens within the app. At the time, this feature emphasized convenience (including automatic conversion of spending) and charged a fixed low fee. By 2021, Revolut had expanded its crypto services to over 15 markets, with a significant increase in its user base (reports indicate millions of users). In 2022, the UK regulator FCA officially approved Revolut's crypto services under anti-money laundering regulations, and the company subsequently launched proof-of-stake token staking services (such as Ethereum 2.0 staking) in the UK and the European Economic Area. Compared to pure cryptocurrency exchanges, Revolut's services are simpler (users do not need to hold private keys, but simply buy and sell at specified prices through the interface) and are natively integrated with its banking applications. In the long term, crypto transaction fees are an important but volatile source of profit – Reuters noted that the "crypto boom" of 2024 significantly boosted the company's profits. However, regulatory policy changes and market cycles have led Revolut to maintain a pragmatic strategy: for example, in 2023, due to scrutiny from the US SEC, it delisted some crypto products in the US.

Current Positioning and Business Metrics

Currently, Revolut conducts its crypto trading business through two main channels: the main application for general users, and the independent advanced trading platform Revolut X for professional users. Revolut X provides a fully functional crypto exchange interface, including zero transaction fees, high liquidity, TradingView charts, and API access, and is marketed under the Revolut brand (slogan: "Crypto Exchange for Professionals"). Assets on Revolut X are held in 1:1 custody (not lent out) and are mostly stored in cold wallets. Meanwhile, the in-app crypto wallet provides instant deposit and withdrawal services for general traders.

EU users trade through Revolut Digital Assets Europe Ltd (an entity licensed by the Cyprus Securities and Exchange Commission and compliant with EU MiCA regulations); UK users access crypto services through Revolut Ltd (a company that has completed FCA anti-money laundering regulatory registration).

The platform supports spot trading of approximately 250 tokens and offers staking services for a limited number of cryptocurrencies, such as Ethereum. Regarding stablecoins, Revolut has not yet launched its own branded stablecoin, but in February 2026, it joined the UK FCA regulatory sandbox to pilot GBP-backed crypto tokens, signaling its intention to enter the stablecoin business. From a business perspective, crypto services fall under the Wealth and Investment segment, alongside stocks and ETFs, and are not an independent profit center. It's worth noting that Revolut does not offer non-custodial self-custodial wallets, thus avoiding competition with hardware wallets or decentralized finance platforms, and consistently positioning itself as a "licensed crypto broker." Currently, publicly available data on Revolut's crypto business is limited; management has not disclosed the number of crypto trading users or their revenue share. However, current indications suggest that while crypto business is an important driver of user activity, it contributes relatively little to revenue: the company's major revenue streams (subscriptions, card fees, foreign exchange fees, and credit interest) all exceed $600 million, yet "cryptocurrency" is not listed as a separate item in the financial statements.

Revolut Stablecoin Monthly Trading Volume

Some indirect data suggests that crypto usage is at a moderate level: for example, the RevPoints loyalty program has 17 million users (who can earn points through crypto-related spending), while the publicly advertised Revolut X customer base represents only a fraction of that total.

Crypto trading volume is likely to fluctuate with market cycles: usage surged during the 2021-2022 bull market, while trading volume declined during the relatively calmer period of 2023-2025. The 2025 annual report highlighted a 45% year-on-year increase in main account usage and growth across almost all product lines, but did not emphasize crypto business growth, indicating that crypto was not a core growth driver last year. Revolut appears to position its crypto capabilities as a compliant distribution channel rather than a speculative side business. With a Crypto Asset Service Provider (CASP) license under the MiCA framework issued by the Cyprus Securities and Exchange Commission, it became one of the first major fintech companies to comply with EU crypto regulatory requirements, enabling it to offer crypto services to retail investors across the European Economic Area. This is expected to make Revolut a one-stop digital asset aggregation platform, effectively connecting traditional financial users with the compliant crypto sector. It is reasonable to infer that the crypto business has increased Revolut's user stickiness (users completing multiple financial activities within a single application) and serves as a customer acquisition and retention tool. However, the crypto business may still represent a limited portion of total revenue. It's not a core profit driver, but rather a value-added feature that allows Revolut's product portfolio to compete with specialized fintech wallets. The crypto business does reinforce Revolut's "super app" positioning, allowing users to quickly acquire digital assets without leaving the platform, and potentially attracting younger or tech-savvy users who would otherwise use exchanges. Nevertheless, Revolut's core narrative remains centered around banking and payments. Unlike specialized exchanges, Revolut doesn't rely on crypto market making or lending (the company claims its assets are not lent out), which limits revenue but also reduces risk. The company's foray into stablecoin pilots and media reports of considering issuing its own token indicate it's exploring new payment channels, but such attempts carry regulatory and reputational risks and are currently small-scale. Looking ahead, Revolut's crypto roadmap appears to be aligned with regulatory policies and will primarily focus on incremental feature expansion. Feasible paths include: completing testing of a pound sterling stablecoin (or other token) in 2026, and considering a formal launch if regulatory approval is obtained; globally promoting Revolut X to gain more trading volume, subject to compliance with local regulations; potentially launching crypto derivatives or decentralized finance (DeFi) entry points, if regulatory permission permits; and continuously integrating crypto assets into loyalty programs or as collateral (e.g., launching crypto-secured loans in the future). Restraining factors include: constantly updated EU MiCA requirements (consumer protection, stablecoin reserves), potentially stricter stablecoin rules in the UK, and intense competition from professional crypto exchanges. For the crypto business to transition from a supplementary business to a significant segment of Revolut's operations, a major structural shift is needed, such as widespread adoption of its own stablecoin or integrating digital asset custody into everyday banking services. This requires both stringent regulatory approval and reliance on user demand. Currently, the crypto business is expected to grow steadily with the market, but its economic contribution may still be relatively limited compared to Revolut's overall banking and payments business. Key Metrics to Watch: **Personal Master Account Ratio:** The percentage of users using Revolut as their primary bank (45% year-over-year growth in 2025) – a key metric for measuring user engagement; **Customer Funds and Loan Size:** Total deposits ($67.5 billion in 2025, up 66% year-over-year) and loan size ($2.9 billion, up 120% year-over-year) – reflecting the funding base and progress towards interest income; **Revenue Growth and Structure:** Total revenue ($6 billion, up 46% year-over-year) and contributions from various segments (e.g., corporate business). 16%; Fee and Interest Income Structure); Trading Volume: Total trading volume (US$1.7 trillion in 2025, up 65% year-on-year) and breakdown of corporate and personal business (corporate business US$365 billion); Profitability: Pre-tax profit margin (38% in 2025) and return on equity – assessing operating leverage; Crypto Business Participation: Percentage of crypto trading users and crypto revenue (not publicly disclosed) – observing business investment signals through new product announcements (new tokens, staking services, etc.); Regulatory Milestones: License updates (full launch of UK business, progress of US licenses, MiCA compliance status) and compliance results (anti-money laundering audit results, etc.) This directly determines the business's growth potential.

Public Facts and Analytical Inferences

In this analysis, we strictly distinguish between publicly disclosed data and analytical inferences. For example, "68.3 million customers by the end of 2025" and "pre-tax profit of £1.7 billion" are factual data from official channels. In contrast, statements regarding the positioning of the crypto business in Revolut's strategy (such as "the crypto business is mainly for user retention and activity") are analytical interpretations based on existing clues (licenses, product integration methods, etc.), and are not information explicitly disclosed by management. In cases of insufficient evidence (such as the unknown revenue share of the crypto business), we have clearly marked the existence of uncertainty. It is important to emphasize that management's publicly expressed optimistic statements (such as the target user base of 100 million by 2027) are only considered as a strategic vision, and we will comprehensively consider them in conjunction with actual performance in our analysis. Conclusion Revolut is now a global fintech giant: no longer the travel card startup of 2015, it has grown into a diversified, large-scale banking platform. With its super-app strategy integrating payments, banking, wealth management, and crypto services, the company has achieved record profitability and massive business scale. Revolut has reached a critical turning point: transforming from a growth-driven fintech company into a mainstream financial institution (as evidenced by the UK's full banking license and achieving break-even with 70 million users). Its core strengths lie in its rich product portfolio, user loyalty, and technology-first cost structure. However, rapid expansion also brings real risks, including compliance issues such as anti-money laundering and capital regulation, as well as cyclical revenue fluctuations affected by the macro environment. While the company's digital asset strategy is clearly defined, it needs to be viewed objectively. Encryption features have strengthened the platform's competitiveness and attracted professional users, but they are only one part of its overall business strategy. Revolut's core focus remains on traditional banking: expanding deposit accounts, extending consumer credit, and consolidating its position in various markets. Therefore, crypto assets and stablecoins are beneficial for the future of finance, but not transformative factors for Revolut's core business. In short, Revolut is growing into a truly global challenger bank, and crypto-related initiatives are strategic supplements—used to enhance user engagement and future business possibilities—rather than the core of its development and transformation.

data.title: Bankless: In-depth analysis of the SEC's new framework for token classification
data.description:
Author: Jack Inabinet, Senior Analyst, Bankless; Translation: @JinseFinancexz

Last week, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) and the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC) jointly released a landmark guidance document, providing a long-awaited joint guidance document that details how and when federal securities laws apply to crypto assets.

While the cryptocurrency industry has long operated in a legal gray area, this new framework attempts to replace some of the uncertainty through a token classification system to help distinguish under what circumstances digital assets qualify as securities.

Today, we will analyze this joint guidance document and speculate on how it might ultimately classify mainstream digital assets.

1. Digital Commodities

According to the SEC's interpretative guidance document, the "digital commodities" category covers crypto assets whose value derives from their "functional" role in the cryptocurrency ecosystem and supply and demand dynamics.

This broad definition has a clear intention to encompass utility tokens from numerous mature blockchain networks.

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has listed 16 crypto assets that fall into this category, as follows: Aptos (APT), Avalanche (AVAX), Bitcoin (BTC), Bitcoin Cash (BCH), Cardano (ADA), Chainlink (LINK), Dogecoin (DOGE), Ether (ETH), Hedera (HBAR), Litecoin (LTC), Polkadot (DOT), Shiba Inu (SHIB), Solana (SOL), Stellar (XLM), Tezos (XTZ), and XRP (XRP). While the SEC's guidance does not provide individual explanations for why these specific cryptocurrencies are classified as non-security digital commodities, it emphasizes that because they do not meet the legal definition of "security," they are not automatically considered securities. The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) claims that its use of the term "digital commodity" is strictly based on an economic meaning to describe the typical characteristics of a class of non-security digital assets. However, the guidance document also states that any crypto asset (excluding stablecoins compliant with the GENIUS Act) may be classified as a genuine "commodity" under the Commodity Exchange Act, subjecting it to independent oversight and regulation by the Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC). Other popular crypto tokens not mentioned by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) but potentially qualifying as "digital commodities" include: BONK: A meme coin on the Solana network, which has evolved to integrate diverse token governance and utility functions since its inception; CC: The native utility token of the Canton blockchain, used for trading, settling transactions, synchronizing data, and transferring assets; EIGEN: A utility token based on a restaking mechanism within the EigenLayer ecosystem, used to provide economic security; ETC: A proof-of-work version of Ethereum, designated by the SEC as a non-security digital commodity; HYPE: Hyperliquid, the native fuel and staking token of the Hyperliquid network, used to pay transaction fees and ensure network security; STX: Stacks, the native fuel and staking reward token of the Stacks Bitcoin Layer 2 network, used to pay transaction fees and ensure network security; SUI: Sui Sui The network's native fuel and staking tokens are used to pay transaction fees and ensure the network's secure operation; TAO: Bittensor, the native token of the Bittensor network, used to incentivize and reward machine learning contributions in the decentralized artificial intelligence market; TRX: Tron, the native fuel and staking token of the Tron network, used to pay transaction fees and ensure the network's secure operation; XMR: Monero, the native token of the privacy-focused Monero network, used to pay transaction fees and ensure the network's secure operation.

 2、Digital Collectibles

Former Chairman of the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission, Gary Gensler, failed to clearly state at a congressional hearing whether tokenized Pokémon cards would be considered securities under federal law, stating that more information was needed to make a final judgment. Now, the cryptocurrency industry seems to have finally received the long-awaited answer.

A newly proposed guidance document from the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) indicates that tokenized Pokémon trading cards may be classified as "digital collectibles," but further information is required before their non-security status can be confirmed. This classification applies to crypto assets intended for collection, including those that "represent or grant" rights to artwork, music, videos, trading cards, in-game items, and previously defined meme-related rights. Similar to physical collectibles, digital collectibles do not grant holders any type of business ownership, even if they possess the right to commercially develop the underlying intellectual property. Therefore, they are naturally considered non-security. The SEC asserts that CryptoPunks, Chromie Squiggles, fan tokens, WIF, and VCOIN can all be classified as digital collectibles. While the SEC classifies memes as digital collectibles, it also states that such tokens could potentially become digital goods once they acquire "functionality" within the associated crypto ecosystem. Furthermore, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission has explicitly stated (as with physical collectibles) that most fragmented collectibles are likely to constitute securities because the buyer relies on the governing efforts of a third party. Perhaps the very necessity of physical custody of the Pokémon cards supporting the tokenized version constitutes a governing effort, thus triggering registration requirements under federal securities laws? Other popular crypto projects not mentioned by the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission but currently potentially qualifying as "digital collectibles" include: Beeple's The First 5000 Days: The highest-priced NFT ever sold, its value stemming from cultural significance, artist reputation, and historical standing in the NFT market; PEPE: A meme coin inspired by internet culture, its value derived from social sentiment and virality; Pudgy Penguins: A community-centric NFT and token brand built around a penguin-themed IP, its value driven by cultural relevance; Quantum Cats: On-chain digital collectibles in the Bitcoin Ordinals series, their value based on scarcity and their position within the Bitcoin ecosystem; Saga Monkes: A limited-edition NFT series tied to the launch of the Solana Saga phone, possessing digital collectible value due to scarcity, early adopter status, and ecosystem relevance.

3. Digital Instruments

Digital instruments are cryptographic assets designed to perform specific functions. While digital instruments may possess intrinsic functional value, many are also “soul-bound,” meaning they cannot be sold and are permanently owned by a single user.

Importantly, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission states that digital instruments must not possess “inherent economic attributes or rights, such as generating passive income or granting claims on future income, profits, or assets of a business, other entity, promisor, or debtor.”

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) has listed a range of use cases for digital instruments, including memberships, tickets, credentials, ownership certificates, and identity tokens. Its guidance document classifies Ethereum domain name service domains and CoinDesk's "Microcosms" NFT consensus conference tickets as digital instruments. Other digital assets that may qualify as "digital instruments" include: FanDome NFT: A collection of NFTs inspired by comic books, distributed free to participants of the DC Comics FanDome virtual conference in 2021; POAP: Digital badges generated in NFT form, minted via blockchain to prove participation in online or offline events; Propy NFT: Digital certificates representing legal ownership of physical real estate, stored on the blockchain; VeeFriends: An NFT series created by Gary Vinerchuk, allowing holders to participate in exclusive events, including the VCON conference; World ID: A soul-bound "proof of identity" certificate issued by Sam Altman's World Coin project.

4. Stablecoins

The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission's interpretative guidance on non-security stablecoins intentionally maintains a concise and direct style, clearly stating that after it takes effect in January next year, it will treat payment-type stablecoins that conform to the description of the GENIUS Act as non-security.

Therefore, the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission itself will not impose registration requirements on payment-type stablecoins that conform to the GENIUS Act during the issuance and redemption process (although these stablecoins themselves will still be subject to extensive supervision by other agencies within the U.S. regulatory system).

However, the guidance document clarifies that prior to the enactment of the GENIUS Act, previously defined "covered stablecoins" were still considered non-securities outside the agency's jurisdiction. For a stablecoin to meet this definition, its holders must not have the right to "receive any interest, profits, or other returns," and its reserves must consist entirely of "US dollars and/or other assets considered low-risk and readily liquid." While the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) does not specifically designate which stablecoins are considered non-securities, instruments pegged to the U.S. dollar that appear to fit its definition of "covered stablecoins" include: PYUSD: A stablecoin issued by Paxos Trust Company, a federally registered national trust bank, for PayPal; USAT: Tether's latest stablecoin pegged to the U.S. dollar, designed to comply with the GENIUS Act; USDC: A stablecoin issued by Circle, over-collateralized 1:1 with U.S. dollars and risk-free U.S. dollar investments; KlarnaUSD: A stablecoin sponsored by Klarna, a buy-now-pay-later giant, and issued by Stripe's Bridge.

5. Digital Securities

It can be said that the most influential guidance document from the U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) is precisely its most ambiguous part.

The regulator maintains that any digital asset that fails the Howe test is considered a security, a determination that has historically involved difficult legal disputes.

The SEC also recognizes that encapsulated versions of financial instruments that are explicitly defined as securities under federal law (such as tokenized money market funds, stocks, and other types of structured investment products) are digital securities.

However, this does not mean that all "encapsulated" tokens are securities.

The guidance document explains that protocol mining, protocol staking, and the "packaging" of non-security crypto assets do not constitute the issuance or sale of securities. The document further clarifies that "certain" airdrops do not constitute "fund investment" under the Howe Test. However, the agency cannot provide a definitive answer as to when a digital asset becomes a digital security. In each case, determining whether an investment contract (i.e., a security) has been issued or sold must be based on the specific facts and circumstances of how the issuer marketed and promoted its asset. If a non-security crypto asset is issued through an investment contract that meets the Howe Test criteria, it will become a security. Furthermore, the initial issuance of a non-security crypto asset through an investment contract does not imply that it is permanently a security; once the original investment contract is fulfilled, or the issuer fails to fulfill its committed operational efforts, the crypto asset can become a non-security. While the SEC refuses to list examples of assets it deems securities, it is noteworthy that its guidance document classifies XRP as a non-security "digital commodity," although courts ultimately ruled that certain sales of the token constituted investment contracts that violated federal securities laws. While this is a step towards regulatory clarity, this aspect of the SEC guidance ultimately reinforces rather than resolves ambiguity. It merely extends existing federal securities laws to the digital asset space, while reigniting market concerns about projects that promise unfinished work or future functionality. Digital goods, digital collectibles, digital instruments, and stablecoins may be non-securities in nature, but they can still be issued or sold through investment contracts, which would subject issuers to federal securities laws.

data.title: x402: Why are Google, Cloudflare, Stripe, and others entering the market?
data.description:
Source: PayAI Network; Compiled by: Jinse Finance Claw

In just over a year, the x402 protocol has transformed from an open-source experiment into a standard supported by tech and payment giants.

This article will delve into who is using x402 for development, why its adoption is accelerating, and what this means for developers and builders positioning themselves in the "Agentic Economy."

Core Summary

  • x402 has strong backing: Google, Cloudflare, Stripe, Visa, Amazon Web Services (AWS), and Circle have all taken action to support or integrate the x402 protocol, signifying that it is no longer in the experimental stage.

  • Formal Foundation Established: Cloudflare and Coinbase jointly established the x402 Foundation in September 2025, with founding members spanning the cloud computing, payments, and infrastructure sectors.

  • AI as a Catalyst: The intelligent agent AI market is projected to grow from $7.3 billion in 2025 to $324 billion in 2034, creating huge demand for “Machine-Native Payment Rails.”

  • Solana Leads Settlement: With fees of less than 1 cent and confirmation speeds of 400 milliseconds, Solana has become the dominant blockchain for high-frequency x402 micropayments.

  • Start Building Now: Open-source SDKs and Facilitator infrastructure, including the Solana-first toolkit from PayAI Network, allow developers to start monetizing their APIs and AI services now.

x402 Adoption is Accelerating: Who's Developing With It

When Coinbase officially released the x402 protocol specification in May 2025, it activated the long-dormant HTTP 402 (Required Payment) status code for stablecoin payments directly embedded in web requests. Within just a few months, its adoption had expanded far beyond the crypto-native community.

By September 2025, Cloudflare and Coinbase co-founded the x402 Foundation, with Google, Visa, AWS, and Circle joining as founding members. Smart agent payments are no longer a future concept; they have now become a cross-industry alliance spanning cloud computing, global payments, and internet infrastructure.

What distinguishes this moment from previous crypto payment attempts is the **convergence**: infrastructure providers, payment processors, and AI platforms are all converging on x402. The internet's payment layer is being restructured for the smart agent era, and the giants are choosing to build it together.

Google and x402: The Search Giant Enters the Internet-Native Payments Market

Google's involvement with x402 goes far beyond simply displaying its logo on the foundation's page. The company has partnered with Cloudflare, Accenture, PayPal, and MetaMask to develop the Agent Payments Protocol (AP2). AP2 positions x402 as the underlying settlement mechanism for agent-initiated purchases and machine-to-machine transactions running on Google Cloud infrastructure.

This is crucial for two reasons: First, Google Cloud is one of the largest platforms for deploying AI workloads, and Gemini-powered agents are becoming increasingly autonomous. These agents need a way to pay for external services (such as data, computing power, and API calls) without human intervention. x402 provides just that: a payment handshake that occurs within a standard HTTP request.

Google's joining Visa, AWS, and Circle in the x402 Foundation sends a clear signal to enterprise buyers: intelligent agent business systems are not just a game for the crypto industry; these companies plan to provide production-grade infrastructure that can collectively serve billions of users. Cloudflare and x402: Payments at the Edge Cloudflare's adoption of x402 is perhaps the most architecturally significant move in the ecosystem. The company launched a **"Pay-per-crawl"** feature, allowing website owners to charge AI bots and crawlers for content access via stablecoin micropayments, turning security vulnerabilities into revenue streams. Since Cloudflare exists as a reverse proxy at the front end of millions of websites, embedding x402 payment support at the network edge means that any site using Cloudflare can monetize its traffic without changing its application code. For developers building applications on Workers or Pages, this eliminates significant friction. Cloudflare CEO Matthew Prince has publicly stated that x402 has the potential to become the foundational internet protocol for machine-to-machine payments, comparing its trajectory to HTTP and HTTPS. When a non-crypto-native infrastructure company with such a massive reach considers payment primitives a top-tier feature, it represents a paradigm shift. Stripe and x402: Traditional Payments Meet a New Standard Stripe's move towards x402 is arguably the strongest confirmation signal for mainstream adoption. The company launched a preview of machine payments integrated with x402, enabling AI agents to use USDC payment API calls and digital services, with payments settled in fiat currency to the merchant's Stripe balance. This "bridging" role is crucial. Stripe processed over $1 trillion in payments in 2024 and is the payment backbone for millions of internet businesses. Its support for x402 means that existing merchants can access stablecoin-based micropayments without rebuilding their payment stack or learning crypto infrastructure. This is a pragmatic entry point for the millions of SaaS platforms, marketplaces, and API providers already using Stripe. They can accept x402 payments from AI agents while continuing to manage their revenue through the familiar Stripe control panel. This is exactly how the protocol scales: by connecting to it where the business is located. AWS Deepens its Commitment to Agent Payments: Integrating x402 into its Core Services To avoid being overtaken by cloud competitors, Amazon Web Services (AWS) has significantly expanded its commitment to the agent economy by integrating x402 support into its Lambda and API Gateway services. In a recent post, AWS announced that developers can now natively configure API Gateway to return 402 status codes for unauthenticated or unpaid requests and leverage AWS Lambda to handle x402 payment negotiation and settlement through the PayAI Network Facilitator. This move significantly reduces friction for AWS's millions of developers monetizing their serverless functions and data streams, positioning x402 as a first-tier feature of their pay-as-you-go cloud service. As a founding member of the x402 Foundation, AWS is providing production-grade tools to ensure that agents running on platforms like Amazon Bedrock can seamlessly pay for resources across the entire cloud ecosystem.

data.title: From Free to Paid: Polymarket's Paid Transition and Growth Flywheel
data.description:
Author: locha, co-founder of Fuul; Translator: xz Jinse Finance

Polymarket's trading volume reached $9.55 billion last month, but generated almost no revenue. This will change on March 30th: the platform will launch charging fees across all ten market categories. The question worth exploring is not whether prediction markets are truly viable, but how Polymarket got to where it is today.

1. Problem: How to launch a market that no one is using

Prediction markets face a severe cold start problem. Without liquidity, traders won't enter; without traders, liquidity providers won't enter—and both sides need to trust the market after it demonstrates accuracy. Accuracy requires trading volume, and trading volume requires the participation of both parties.

Two years ago, the entire prediction market industry had only about 4,000 monthly active users and an annual trading volume of $15.8 billion. Today, Polymarket alone has a monthly trading volume of $9.55 billion. The industry's total trading volume is projected to reach $63.5 billion in 2025, a fourfold increase year-over-year. In February 2026, Polymarket and Kalshi's combined monthly trading volume reached $16.8 billion, pushing the industry's annualized trading volume towards $200 billion. How did they grow from 4,000 users to this scale? The answer isn't just better products, but also incentives.

2、Core Concept: Adopting Mature Strategies of Cryptocurrency Exchanges

Polymarket did not create a new incentive model, but directly adopted the growth primitives that cryptocurrency exchanges have already proven at a trillion-dollar scale.

  • Referral Programs: Binance, OKX, and Bybit have all built massive user bases through referral commission mechanisms, offering 10%-50% of trading fees to any user who brings in a new trader. Binance used this to expand to 280 million users and $20 billion in daily trading volume. These programs turn every user into a distribution channel.

  • Builder Code: Hyperliquid pioneered this model in the DeFi space: opening up APIs to developers, enabling them to route orders, attribute trading volume, and earn fees. In July 2025, the daily revenue of builder code on Hyperliquid exceeded $10 million, with applications like pvp[.]trade and Axiom driving a large volume of trading through their own interfaces. Market Maker Rebates: All major exchanges redistribute a portion of their order book fees to market makers. Subsidizing liquidity providers to narrow spreads and increase trading volume is a classic technique in exchange design. Polymarket was the first prediction market to simultaneously run all three mechanisms. However, they did not launch all mechanisms at once, but implemented them in phases over four years, with each phase addressing a specific bottleneck issue.

    Phase 1: Starting Liquidity from Zero (2022-2023)

    In early 2022, Polymarket launched a cycle-based liquidity mining program: with a 7-day cycle, approximately 50,000 USDC and 10,000 UMA were distributed to all users providing liquidity in each cycle. Rewards were calculated per block to ensure the order book remained active 24/7.

    ... By March 2023, the program was upgraded to a formal market maker rewards program, distributing rewards daily based on spread tightness and order book depth. By early 2025, the estimated cumulative subsidies had reached tens of millions of USDC. The goal is simple: to incentivize people to create liquidity, making the product available. Without liquidity, there is no price discovery; without price discovery, the product cannot exist. Phase Two: Capturing Event-Driven Demand Through Aggressive Subsidies (Late 2023 - Early 2025) Polymarket has invested over $10 million in direct USDC incentives across hundreds of markets, building upon the core market maker program with accelerated incentives for specific events. Temporary high-yield pools and volume-based rebates are available for elections, cryptocurrency cycles, and major geopolitical events. This is pure customer acquisition spending, supported by a treasury. While costly, the timing is precise. The 2024 US presidential election will bring prediction markets into the mainstream. In November 2024 alone, Polymarket attracted nearly 300,000 users to participate in betting, totaling over $2.5 billion. Polymarket's ability to offer the deepest order book depth is due to its two-year commitment to subsidizing liquidity. Phase Three: Introducing a Yield Mechanism to Retain Funds (September 2025) Attracting traders to deposit funds is one issue; keeping them on the platform between events is another. Holding Rewards will launch on September 24, 2025: providing an annualized 4% USDC yield on open positions in selected markets, paid out daily. Sampling positions hourly means rewards are linked to the persistence of holdings, not just trading activity. Polymarket borrowed a fundamental DeFi model (deposit rewards) and applied it to prediction markets. Around the same time, an initial version of the referral program quietly launched: new users who deposited funds through a referral code received a 10 USDC credit reward. This simple, low-friction entry method aimed to lower the cold start barrier for individual users, not just the market itself. Phase Four: Launching the Fee Engine (January-March 2026) At this point, the model began to become self-sustaining. January 5, 2026: First, taker fees were implemented in the 15-minute cryptocurrency prediction market (peaking at 1.56% with a 50% probability). A market maker rebate program was launched simultaneously, funded by these fees. The key shift was that takers now provided funding for market makers. Liquidity is no longer subsidized by the treasury. Mid-February: Fee and rebate mechanisms expanded to sports (college basketball, Serie A) and shorter-cycle cryptocurrency markets. February 17: Sponsored liquidity rewards opened to all users. Any user can deposit USDC into a smart contract that automatically rewards liquidity providers in any market of their choice. This democratizes the incentive mechanism. Hedge funds, institutions, and retail investors can now launch liquidity in niche markets without Polymarket involvement. March 6: Fees fully covered all cryptocurrency markets (1-hour, 4-hour, daily, weekly). March 17-19: Over $2 million in targeted liquidity rewards provided for "March Madness". Each NCAA Men's Basketball Tournament match offers a $100,000 prize pool, with an estimated $6 million to be awarded throughout the tournament. **March 23-24:** Two measures are implemented simultaneously. Market maker rebates are expanded to almost all new markets. The full referral program is open to all traders with a lifetime trading volume exceeding $10,000: 30% commission on takers' fees from direct referrals and 10% commission on second-level referrals, valid for 180 days and continuing until December 2027. **March 30:** Fee markets expand from two categories to ten. Categories such as Politics, Finance, Economics, Culture, Weather, and Technology begin generating revenue. The Builder Code program continues to operate during this period, providing developers with an API to route orders, attribute trading volume, and earn weekly USDC rewards. Even before the referral program even launches, aggregators like Dome (acquired by Polymarket in early 2026) and open-source tools like pmxt are already built on this infrastructure. The Builder Code program continues to run during this period, providing developers with APIs to route orders, attribute trade volume, and earn weekly USDC rewards. Even before the referral program even launches, aggregators like Dome (acquired by Polymarket in early 2026) and open-source tools like pmxt are already built on this infrastructure. 3. No points, no tasks, no tokens. Only USDC. Every incentive running on Polymarket is paid in USDC. Liquidity rewards, market maker rebates, holding rewards, referral commissions—all settled in the same stablecoin traders use for trading. This is a well-considered choice. This means that each incentive has a clear, immediately visible cost and a measurable return on investment. This means Polymarket can calculate its unit economics like a real business, rather than a growth experiment relying on token subsidies. It also means that the transition from treasury-funded subsidies to fee-funded incentives is clear and smooth: the same currency, the same mechanism, different funding sources.

    From "paying people to participate" to "using fees to keep people engaged," this complete path took four years. Most cryptocurrency projects have never completed this transition. Polymarket did.

    4. Current Data Overview (and why this is the bottom line, not the top line)

    So what does all this mean? Castle Labs did the calculations: taking the transaction volume of each category over the most recent 30 days, applying the new fee structure, and estimating revenue.

    This is a benchmark, not a prediction. These markets have already reached such trading volumes before fees were introduced and referral programs were launched. The incentive flywheel hasn't even started turning yet. But the following data illustrates its conservatism: the referral program just launched on March 23rd. For the first time, every opinion leader, key opinion leader, media company, and content creator in the cryptocurrency space has a direct financial incentive to drive users to Polymarket: 30% of the taker's commission for direct referrals and 10% for second-level referrals, paid daily in USDC. This is the same mechanism that previously transformed crypto Twitter, YouTube, and trading academies into distribution machines for Binance and Bybit. The only difference is that the product is no longer a leveraged perpetual contract exchange, but something that people outside the crypto space would genuinely want to use.
