Imagine what might happen next: a political podcast releasing a Polymarket referral link during election season; a sports media account including a referral code when embedding market odds predictions; a financial news email linking to an economic forecasting market. Now, these promoters earn real revenue from every trader they bring in. The referral program will convert Polymarket's entire audience into a paid sales team, and the potential user base of prediction markets is orders of magnitude larger than that of cryptocurrency trading.

    5. Conclusion

    The cryptocurrency industry has spent over a decade answering the question, "What's the point?" Trading and speculation were the first undisputed answer. Stablecoins followed closely. Prediction markets are becoming the next answer.

    The launch of fees on March 30th is not a trivial footnote; it's setting off a super-fast growth flywheel.

data.title: The AI ​​industry is lying to you.
data.description:
Author: Edward Zitron, Compiled: Block unicorn

Foreword

The entire AI bubble is built on a

While all of this is very strange and suspicious, I must be clear: there is currently no definitive answer to explain all of this except that NVIDIA GPUs did indeed somehow flow into China. I believe it's impossible that Jensen Huang was completely unaware of this, and it's almost impossible for multi-billion dollar GPUs to appear in places where NVIDIA was unaware.

In short, Supermicro CEO Charles Liang has not yet commented on Wally Liaw's comments.

In short, Supermicro CEO Charles Liang has not yet commented on Wally Liaw's comments.

Or comments from their alleged accomplices, with the company only issuing a statement saying their actions “violated the company’s policies and compliance controls.” It seems no one has asked Jensen Huang if he knew about this—neither Megaspeed, Supermicro, nor anyone in the past few years has asked him any challenging questions. However, Huang stated in May 2025 that “there is no evidence of any AI chip transfer,” and that the countries involved “will be very careful in their self-monitoring.” For legal reasons, I must speak very cautiously: I cannot say Huang did anything wrong or lied, but I find it unbelievable, even absurd, that he was completely unaware of this. Really? According to a December 2025 report in *The Information*, hundreds of millions, even billions of dollars worth of GPUs are flowing into China, and Huang was completely unaware? I think it's highly unlikely, though of course, I can't be entirely sure. If Nvidia really knew—of course, I'm not saying they actually knew—it's an absolute huge scandal, but aside from a few brave individuals at The Information and Bloomberg who genuinely care about the truth, almost no one's paying attention. Has anyone asked Jensen Huang? He's a frequent face on camera. **P.S.:** Earlier today, Senators Jim Banks and Elizabeth Warren wrote to Trump's Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, requesting the Commerce Department take "all necessary and appropriate measures" to prevent Nvidia chips from flowing to China, including a possible ban on exports to countries considered intermediaries such as Malaysia, Thailand, Vietnam, and Singapore. Liaw's arrest seems to have caused some controversy in Washington, and I wouldn't be surprised if Jensen Huang testifies before Congress at some point. **I'd also like to add that what shocks me is how many people are dismissing Supermicro and moving on.** But it's a huge joke that the major providers of the two mainstream cloud platforms (Crusoe and CoreWeave) and a smaller cloud platform (Lambda, to which they also rent cloud capacity) are unaware that several percentage points of their revenue are flowing directly to China through one of their co-founders. I hope we can eventually find out the truth. Nevertheless, this despicable tactic almost exposes the desperation of everyone involved. The End of Software Engineering: Hyperscale Data Center Operators Force Employees to Use AI, Leading to Degraded Product Quality and Service Crashes. So, I want to explain something very clearly to you, because you need to understand how bad things are: Hyperscale data center operators are forcing everyone in the company to use AI tools as much as possible, tying salaries and performance to token consumption, and actively encouraging non-technical personnel to write features actually for production use. In reality, this means everyone is expected to spend all day fiddling with AI tools and consuming large amounts of tokens, and for designers at some companies, they're even expected to actively write features without any coding knowledge. "How did I know the last part? Because a reliable source told me—I'll stop here." People might mistakenly believe this signifies a leap forward in AI efficiency, but the reality is a host of incomplete internal dashboards that spend hours measuring random user data or converting files just to theoretically save a few minutes. While non-technical personnel aren't necessarily allowed to deploy code directly to production, their poorly written pseudo-software—written without any technical knowledge—is then "fixed" by real software engineers who are equally responsible for fulfilling their duties. These tools also allow mediocre software engineers to cause more damage than ever before. At least in one hyperscale data center, LLM usage was relatively unrestricted (even actively incentivized), with almost anyone able to launch their own OpenClaw "AI agent" (actually a series of LLMs claiming to perform various operations on your inbox or Slack, but offering no apparent benefit other than deleting all your emails). In Meta's case, this ultimately led to a serious security breach. According to internal Meta communications and incident reports seen by *The Information*, a major security alert occurred last week. A Meta software engineer used an internal intelligent agent tool similar to OpenClaw to analyze a technical issue posted by another Meta employee on an internal forum. Internal communications state that after the analysis, the AI ​​agent responded to the original question on the forum and offered advice on the technical issue. All of this was done without the employee's authorization. According to *The Information*, Meta systems store a large amount of company and user-related data, which unauthorized engineers could access. The incident was marked as a sec-1 incident, the second highest level in Meta's internal ranking system for security incidents. This incident followed multiple issues with Amazon's Kiro and Q LLM systems. The following is a report by Eugene King of *Business Insider*: March 2 On March 5th, another system glitch caused a 99% drop in orders on Amazon's North American marketplace, resulting in the loss of 6.3 million orders. An internal document revealed that Amazon's AI tool Q was one of the main causes of the incident. LLM is destroying Big Tech from within. Despite the overwhelming (and exhausting) marketing campaigns surrounding the "power of AI code," I believe these incidents are just the beginning of the real consequences of AI coding tools: the slow disintegration of the tech industry's software stack. Even the most incompetent idiot can be mimicked by an LLM engineer. I mean, you can tell it, "Write me some software that can do this" or "Look at this code and fix it," and the LLM will keep saying, "You can do it" and "That's a great solution." The problem is, while an LLM can write "all" code, that doesn't mean the code is good, or that someone can read it and understand its intent (because these models don't think), or that having a lot of code is a good thing now and in the future for any company built using generative code. LLM-based code is often verbose and rarely conforms to internal coding guidelines and standards, guaranteeing a longer reading time. This naturally means that those responsible for reviewing it either just skim it or hand it over to another LLM to figure out exactly what to do. Worse still, the use of LLMs is completely directionless. Why would people at Meta use it? OpenClaw? Besides burning through a huge amount of tokens, what's the point of OpenClaw? Consider this: You explicitly instruct all engineers in your company to write code using LLM tokens, and incentivize them to do so by tracking LLM usage. Now, you've significantly increased your company's operating costs (costs consumed through tokens) and code creation. To be clear, letting LLM write all your code means you stop writing code, stop learning how to code, and won't become a better software engineer as a result. This means that almost all large tech companies are encouraging software engineers to stop learning how to write software or solve software architecture problems. If you're just someone who looks at code, your skill level depends on the quality of the code generated by the model. As Mo Bitar... As discussed recently, these models are designed to motivate, confuse, and tell you how great you are, when you only glance at a bunch of rewritten code that, even if it works, eventually evolves into a single entity whose purpose and intent are merely code generated by the model based on your prompts. Worse still, mega-corporations like Meta and Amazon are keen on laying off thousands at a time, making it harder to understand why things were built the way they were. This becomes even more complicated when an LLM built with a lack of thought and intent. Many trillion-dollar companies have code written with these things, and the writers (engineers and non-engineers alike!) may be gone a month or a year later, unable to explain what prompts they used. We've already seen the consequences! Amazon lost hundreds of thousands of orders! Meta... A major security breach has occurred! The foundations of these companies are being eroded by millions of lines of poorly written code, code that at best occasionally earns the approval of someone whose resume says "software engineer"—and who is frequently fired. This increases the likelihood that someone who truly understands the situation or knows why something is built that way can prevent things from going wrong. Remember: Google, Amazon, Microsoft, and Meta all possess vast amounts of personal information, private conversations, critical legal documents, financial information, and in some cases even Social Security numbers. These four companies, and a worrying large portion of the tech industry, are actively encouraging their software engineers to disregard the software itself. Oh, you're coding much faster with AI? What does that actually mean? What exactly did you build? Do you understand how it works? Did you review the code before it went live, or did you assume it was fine just because it didn't have any errors? This is creating a biblical plague in the field of software engineering—the entire tech industry is built on massive amounts of unmanageable and unintentional code driven by managers and executives who are not doing their jobs. LLM allows the incompetent to masquerade as capable, inefficient individuals to produce work-related outputs stemming from a dislike of labor and craft, and fosters the malpractices of Silicon Valley's mediocrity. Everything in Silicon Valley seems to revolve around growth; "more" is often equated with "more valuable." Kevin Roose of The New York Times—in a strikingly journalistic style—recently wrote an article praising the competition within Silicon Valley to burn ever more tokens using AI models: An OpenAI engineer last week processed 210 billion "tokens" (enough to fill Wikipedia 33 times) using the company's AI model, the highest processing volume among all employees. Anthropic's AI coding system, Claude Code, generated over $150,000 in fees within a month. At tech companies like Meta and Shopify, managers have begun incorporating AI usage into performance reviews, rewarding employees who heavily utilize AI tools and criticizing those who don't. This has become the new normal for programmers, among the first white-collar workers to feel the impact of AI on the economy. AI was originally intended to help tech companies increase productivity and reduce costs. But it has also spawned a costly new identity game known as "token maximization," where AI-obsessed employees are eager to prove their productivity. Roose explains that Meta and OpenAI both have internal leaderboards showing how many tokens you've used; one software engineer in Stockholm "spent more on tokens than his salary," though Roose adds that these tokens... It was paid for by the company. Roose described a morbid corporate culture where OpenAI rewards those who spend huge sums on tokens. He added that he had spoken with several tech workers who spent thousands of dollars a day buying tokens "just to show off." Roose added an even more bizarre detail: someone exploited a vulnerability in Claude's $20 monthly subscription using software developed by Figma, burning through $70,000 worth of tokens. Despite all this spending, Roose struggled to find anyone who could explain what they were doing besides "using parallel-running coding agents to maintain large, complex software," but he eventually found one particularly useful piece of information—it might all be a performance: They generally believe that AI coding tools have improved their productivity. However, some see the use of AI as a strategic move—to demonstrate to colleagues and bosses that they are up-to-date, as the era of manual coding seems to be coming to an end. I do give Roose some credit, because he questions, "...whether these token maximizers are producing anything good, or are they just wasting their time churning out useless code to try and appear busy?" That said, I still find this story chilling and dangerously close to the behavior of drug addicts and cult members. In this report published in one of the world's largest-circulation newspapers, Roose couldn't find a single "token maximizer" who could truly describe their work, consistent with my past experiences assessing those who incessantly talk about the power of "proxy coding." These people are sick; they are participating in a wicked, toxic culture based on unnecessary spending and endless consumption. Companies that encourage users to burn tokens are actually creating a culture of productivity through waste and fostering a destructive tendency—one stemming from users constantly looking for things to do rather than doing things purposefully. In their pursuit of so-called "artificial intelligence," simply because other companies are doing it, they are destined to make their software crudely written and poorly maintained. Anyone who has actually worked knows that those who appear most efficient are often the most useless, because everything they do is to appear efficient, not to produce any truly valuable results. A recent Business Insider interview with someone who was laid off from Amazon after learning about "artificial intelligence" and "atmosphere coding" is a good example. He was surprised to find that these so-called skills did not save him from being laid off: "During the October layoffs, everyone was discussing whether artificial intelligence was a reason for the layoffs." At the time, the company encouraged us to use artificial intelligence, but I didn't think it took my job. I was responsible for writing internal product descriptions at Amazon, and when using AI, I needed it to remove some irrelevant words to make it sound like a human. Despite my concerns, I used AI, but in my opinion, it was far from replacing my job. Before being laid off, I participated in the AI ​​development of Amazon's internal website. I had never really touched programming before, but with the help of a colleague, I learned how to code through trial and error. I felt that using AI in this project and showcasing my different skills would make me more valuable, but ultimately, it didn't save me from being laid off. To be clear, this person was a victim. Amazon forced him to learn useless skills, to create useless things in an expensive and inefficient way, and despite being pressured to use tools he didn't like, he ultimately lost his job. Note: If you read this and think she should make better use of artificial intelligence, you've been fooled. You're being tricked into doing unpaid marketing work for AI companies that actually hate you. This person was actively involved in the AI ​​development of Amazon's internal website and had to "learn Vibe Code through a lot of trial and error and with the help of colleagues." Was it worth it for her? Was it worth it for her colleagues? No! In fact, in all these damn AI coding hype Twitter accounts and endless pronouncements about the powerful capabilities of AI agents, I find almost no record of anything substantial other than someone saying, "Yeah, I seem to be more efficient." I'm convinced that in the near future, some major tech service will suffer from an unfixable glitch caused by thousands of people using AI coding tools to develop software, compounded by layoffs and a corporate culture that encourages employees to pretend to be busy rather than actually create useful things, further exacerbating the talent drain. What else can you expect? You give people a number they can improve, a number that makes them look like they're doing a better job, and what do you think they'll do? Try to increase their efficiency? Or make the most of those numbers, even if it's not necessary? I haven't even talked about how expensive all this actually is, partly because it's hard to fully grasp. But what I do know is that big tech companies are putting themselves through crisis after crisis, especially when Anthropic and OpenAI stopped subsidizing their models in ways that allowed people to spend $2,500 or more on a $200 monthly subscription. What about those who rely on these models? What about those who have forgotten how to work because they've let AI write all the code? Will they still be able to work? Large language models are creating Silicon Valley's Habsburg families—employees who become intellectually trapped once they start relying on them. These models are so heavily subsidized that their bosses encourage them to use them as much as possible. While they might be able to return to the workforce, a software engineer who has only used a large language model for more than a few months will have to relearn basic work habits and find their skills limited to the last training version of the model they recently used. I believe there are many software engineers who use these models ethically; they read all the code, have a complete understanding of the industry, and use it as a means to handle specific units of work they have complete control over. I'm also sure there are some who simply get the program to do something, glance at the code, and release it. We can't count the number of people in either category, but hearing Spotify's CEO say that their top developers basically stop writing code deeply worries me because this doesn't replace software engineering at all—it's just blindly eliminating friction and imposing "good" or "correct" standards on users, and Spotify... However, this is deliberately exaggerated. Ultimately, this era tests a person's ability to understand and appreciate friction. Friction can sometimes be a good thing. When I encounter problems I don't understand, I try my best to figure them out, and once I grasp them, the feeling is simply wonderful. Over the past three years, I've had to self-study a great deal about finance, accounting, and the broader technology industry, and I've been frustrated countless times by things I couldn't understand, even doubting whether I could truly comprehend certain things. I have plenty of time, but unfortunately, many software engineers face increasingly insane deadlines set by bosses who know nothing about software development, let alone the capabilities of LLM or what responsible software engineering is. The pressure from superiors to use these models simply because they are "faster than humans writing code" is a disastrous confusion between "fast" and "good," all stemming from the hype surrounding LLMs peddled by venture capitalists and the media. The myth that "any code can be written" is untenable. Generative code is a digital ecosystem disaster, and because companies are required to write as much code as possible as quickly as possible, this disaster will take years to repair. Everyone involved must be held accountable, especially those poorly managed software companies, who will ultimately suffer the disastrous consequences of building software on sand. The tech industry has poisoned itself with the lies of AI. Ultimately, everything about AI is built on lies. Hundreds of gigabytes of data centers under development are actually equivalent to only 5 gigabytes under construction. Hundreds of billions of dollars in GPU sales are mostly idle, waiting to be used. Anthropic's consistently high annualized revenue eventually reached $5 billion within four years, while its salaries and computing expenses totaled $25 billion or more. Despite the sheer number of data centers reportedly being built, it seems no one is profiting from renting out AI computing resources. AI's supposed ability to "write all code" actually means that all large software companies are churning out junk code in their codebases while operating costs are skyrocketing. Software engineers haven't been replaced—they've been laid off because the software designed to replace them is too expensive, not because they've actually replaced anyone. A little deeper into the industry reveals that we are witnessing one of the worst business failures in history. Those self-satisfied, condescending AI advocates exist solely to blind you to the harsh truth—AI... It generates almost no benefit, lacks tangible productivity gains, and in the long run, it seems likely to harm the productivity and efficiency of the employees forced to use it. Any executive who forces employees to use AI is a cold-blooded idiot who has no idea what real work is like and is likely a lazy, selfish moron. Everyone I've met at large tech companies is frustrated; they're constantly pressured to "embrace AI," launch more products, and do more things, but there's no real definition of what "more" actually means or what it contributes to the overall picture. Meanwhile, they're constantly worried about being laid off because a very harmful corporate culture is brewing around these services. It's poison for the future of the tech industry. It's costly, inefficient, and will damage users' learning abilities and work efficiency, depriving them of opportunities to learn and grow, hindering their progress, and ultimately leading them to mechanically repeat instructions, lacking knowledge and declining skills. Those who cheer for artificial intelligence are either ignorant, cowardly, bribed, cunning, or eager to be pioneers of the next era—even if that era is terrible, even if it's illogical, even if a little thought reveals that it's simply impossible. Ultimately, AI is a test of your introspection. Can you tell when you truly understand something? Can you explain why you believe something, and not just because someone tells you to believe it, or because you feel guilty for holding a different view? Do you genuinely want to know the truth, or do you merely possess the ability to access information when needed? How much joy do you derive from becoming a better person? If you can't answer that question definitively, perhaps you should just use LLM, because you don't really care about anything. Ultimately, you're the perfect sucker for the AI ​​industry, which can't sell itself without fabricating lies to market what it can (or theoretically can) do.

data.title: The Art of Crypto Compliance
data.description:
Author: DTA; Source: X,@BTCDTA

The world was still racing along its magical yet real trajectory when I opened my eyes this morning. In Seoul, Microsoft's AI conference was in full swing, showcasing how Copilot evolved from an auxiliary chat tool into a digital employee capable of independent thought. Meanwhile, Japanese auto giants Sony and Honda suddenly announced the shelving of their Afeela electric car project. This contrast is quite interesting: the tide of technology is frantically retreating from a comprehensive hardware narrative towards deep and sophisticated AI computing power and sovereign models.

Looking at Crypto in this context, you'll find the same logic: **Rules are becoming increasingly specific, and the wisdom of survival lies in how to encapsulate complexity within simple definitions.** The hottest topic for large investors recently isn't which altcoin is rebounding, nor which market maker is going bankrupt, but rather the fact that **US stablecoin rules are starting to refine the definition of stablecoins, moving beyond simply allowing issuance to specify whether returns can be offered, how those returns are offered, and who will offer them.** **Regulators are no longer just monitoring stablecoins themselves, but rather how to draw the line between stablecoins and returns.** Many people interpret this as negative for CRCL, but I think they're only seeing the surface. The real issue isn't whether stablecoins will still allow users to receive benefits in the future, but who will offer those benefits, whether those benefits are tied to asset balances or platform behavior, and ultimately, what they will be called in legal terms. The problem with the structure of Circle and Coinbase isn't the products themselves or the lack of asset transparency, but rather their proximity to the US regulatory center. **Proximity to the regulatory center has one advantage: a clear compliance path. However, it also has a disadvantage: when regulators want to find a case, they're the first to target you.** Especially when discussions begin to focus on whether holding stablecoins can directly generate interest, the projects most resembling deposits and most likely to be targeted by the traditional banking system are not peripheral projects, but rather the most central, standardized, and seemingly legitimate extensions of formal finance. Therefore, it's not surprising that CRCL was initially suppressed by the market. The market isn't trading on the wording of a bill, but on an expectation: **Will the US prioritize addressing stablecoin yields from those most resembling interest rates?** If this expectation holds true, the closer a model is to the US regulatory narrative, the faster it will be discounted. Looking at USD1 from the opposite perspective, its current path is even more interesting. Instead of telling the story of passively earning USD interest by holding the token, it places more emphasis on incentives through platform activities, ecosystem collaboration, and promotional rewards. Recently, holding USD1 on Binance to earn WLFI essentially provides users with a tangible sense of return, but the narrative doesn't directly address the USD interest rate issue. This is a layer many people overlook. Regulators never just look at whether you've issued funds, but more importantly, why you've issued funds and under what name. If it's a fixed return on a balance basis, it's more like interest in law; if it's an incentive centered around platform operations, user behavior, and ecosystem promotion, then at least at this stage, it's more like a promotion. The user experience might be similar, but from a regulatory perspective, they're completely different. Therefore, my current judgment is simple: CRCL's product logic didn't suddenly collapse; rather, it became too close to the center of the rules, making it a target for stress testing. USD1 hasn't received a permanent immunity from punishment; it's just temporarily stuck in a smarter position, circumventing the most direct legal conflict through platform incentives and name design. This is why I've always felt that the true power of compliance isn't about making yourself a smooth, unyielding rock, but about finding a buffer zone within the boundaries of the rules—a buffer that allows you to breathe, grow, and still benefit users. This buffer zone often determines whether a project's valuation is slashed by the market first, or whether it can run a little further.

data.title: Global AI companies have burned through hundreds of billions, but in the end, they all have to scramble to "work" for Apple.
data.description: The global AI arms race is a real battle, a fight to the death. OpenAI, Google, Anthropic—any one of them has poured tens of billions of dollars into computing power, talent salaries, and model training over the past two years. In contrast, Apple, for years, has seemed like an outsider, failing to deliver a decent large-scale model and even being ridiculed for falling behind in the AI ​​wave. However, this doesn't mean Apple has been inactive in the face of the AI ​​revolution. Just now, according to Bloomberg, iOS 27 will introduce a new mechanism called "Extensions," allowing users to integrate third-party AI such as Google Gemini and Anthropic Claude into Siri through the settings panel, just like making a request directly from Siri now when calling ChatGPT. In short, Siri aims to become the "entry point" for AI applications. The beta system contains an internal message: "Extensions allow agents from installed apps to work with Siri, the Siri app, and other features on your devices." This mechanism is fundamentally different from the previous exclusive partnership model of ChatGPT. ChatGPT was a special version negotiated by Apple, on a case-by-case basis, which was time-consuming and laborious. Extensions, on the other hand, is a standard protocol that allows any qualified AI application to access it. In other words, Siri's intelligence will be increasingly outsourced to third-party AI providers. Everything is ready, just waiting to collect rent. Actually, Apple Intelligence was never designed as a single large model system from the beginning, but rather a "three-layer decoupled" architecture: The first layer is the device side. A lightweight model with approximately 3 billion parameters runs locally on the iPhone, handling text summarization, fast conversations, and privacy-sensitive operations. This model is specially trained with 2-bit quantization and deeply optimized for Apple chips, making it fast, offline, and ensuring data remains on the device. The second layer is the private cloud. Apple's self-developed server-side large model uses a PT-MoE (Parallel Track Hybrid Expert) architecture to handle complex reasoning and long texts. Apple claims that this cloud computing environment uses end-to-end encryption, data is only used to process requests and not stored, and the operating environment can even be reviewed by third-party experts. This system, called "Private Cloud Compute," is the source of Apple's confidence on privacy issues. The third layer is the third-party model layer. Third-party AI is accessed through standardized interfaces, becoming a "replaceable engine" for the entire system. The key to this architecture is the decoupling of the three layers. The upper-level tool calls, permission management, and user interface are completely unconcerned about which model is running underneath. The user perceives "Siri did this," but who is actually doing the work is determined by the system. There is another crucial prerequisite for opening up to third-party AI. There are several paths for third-party AI to enter the Siri ecosystem. One is the App Intents framework, which allows developers to expose their app's functionality to the system's AI, enabling cross-app calls; the other is the Model Context Protocol (MCP), which Apple is making system-level support for, integrating with the App Intents framework. MCP isn't Apple's invention; it's an open protocol emerging in the AI ​​industry, with Anthropic being one of its early proponents. Apple's choice to support MCP at the system level means that, theoretically, any AI service conforming to this protocol can be uniformly scheduled by Siri. For developers, Apple has also opened up the Foundation Models Framework. This is an SDK that allows native access to Apple Intelligence's core models using Swift, reportedly requiring only 3 lines of code. It includes guided generation, restricted tool calls, and LoRA adapter fine-tuning. In short, even if developers can build on Apple's foundation, the rules of that foundation are still set by Apple. OpenAI's exclusive honeymoon has ended. Recall that ChatGPT's integration into Siri was a highlight of Apple Intelligence's announcement at WWDC 2024. However, this collaboration wasn't entirely harmonious within Apple. Former Apple AI head John Giannandrea held reservations about OpenAI at the time, questioning the company's long-term competitiveness and preferring to collaborate with Google. Ultimately, Apple held an internal "AI competition," and ChatGPT emerged victorious, hailed as "the best choice at the time." In retrospect, the phrase "at the time" carries significant weight. Now, with the Extensions mechanism in place, ChatGPT's exclusive status has naturally crumbled. Ideally, users would be able to choose which AI to use to answer questions: Gemini, Claude, Grok, Copilot, or Perplexity. Siri could use any of these as long as the corresponding app was installed. ChatGPT would still be available, but it wouldn't be the only option. It's worth noting that Google's stock price fell on the day the news broke. This reaction seems contradictory at first glance—Gemini is about to be integrated into Siri, shouldn't Google be happy? But upon closer inspection, this opening of Siri Extensions is different from Apple's previous project of "rebuilding the underlying Siri system using the Gemini model." The latter involves Apple adopting Gemini's model capabilities at the technical level, which is the real big deal; the former involves integrating Gemini's client application into Siri, which is on par with integrating Claude and ChatGPT. There's also a deeper concern. According to previously disclosed information, Apple retains access to Gemini's model weights in its collaboration with Google, using them to build its own basic model capabilities. In other words, while Apple brought Gemini in as an "equal tenant," it was also building its own foundation using Google's technological expertise. When Apple uses Extensions to treat all AI on an equal footing, Gemini loses its priority in securing the larger opportunity of having the "exclusive underlying layer for Siri," significantly diluting its negotiating leverage. This is the logic behind the price drop. Furthermore, this openness is part of Apple's broader Siri restructuring plan, which also includes: a standalone Siri app, a new Siri interface, integration of Siri and Spotlight search, and new entry points such as "Ask Siri" and "Write with Siri." These features are planned for official release at this year's WWDC, but may still be subject to change or delays. Slow and steady wins the race. Following the Bloomberg report, a user on the X platform commented: "Siri is now the world's number one AI, but it hasn't run any models on its own, nor has it spent a penny on training. Claude, ChatGPT, and Gemini have to be integrated to reach Siri's 2.5 billion users. Then Apple charges 30% on subscriptions through the App Store. The AI ​​just runs on iOS like that, and Apple barely lifts a finger." This statement is entirely logically sound. Apple's platform strategy has always been this: control the operating environment and distribution channels, let others compete on it, and collect the tolls. The App Store has already profited from the mobile internet industry in this way, and now this logic is being repeated in the AI ​​industry. Apple is already taking a cut through ChatGPT subscriptions: when users pay for ChatGPT Plus on their iPhones, the App Store payment system participates, and Apple receives a share. With the Extensions mechanism expanding, every paid AI service integrated into Siri, such as Gemini and Claude, will become a new revenue-sharing channel. The fiercer the AI ​​war, the more user subscriptions Apple gains, ultimately reaping the benefits. In contrast, looking back over the past two years, the competitive script for AI companies has been highly homogenized: releasing new models, setting new benchmarks, grabbing media headlines, achieving higher scores and valuations, then releasing the next new model. However, this "involution" in underlying computing power and benchmark scores masks the industry's biggest pain point:

data.title: a16z: Why the next billion AI users will access the internet through trusted networks
data.description:
Author: Sakina Arsiwala, a16z Researcher; Source: a16z crypto; Translated by: Shaw Jinse Finance

The Lesson of YouTube: Content as a Geopolitical Weapon

Years ago, I was the head of international search products at Google, and subsequently led YouTube's international expansion, launching the product in 21 countries in just 14 months. What I did was not just localize the product, but also build local content partnerships, finding ways through the minefield of laws, policies, and market access. More recently, I was also responsible for the community health (trust and safety) management of Twitch. Throughout my career, I have also founded two startups.

The current state of artificial intelligence (AI) bears a striking resemblance to the early growth phases of Google and YouTube. My career has taught me that **globalization is not a product feature, but a geopolitical game**. The most profound lesson is that channel expansion is never purely a technical issue. Growth relies on local partners, cultural communicators, and trusted community opinion leaders who build bridges between global platforms and local users. I witnessed the GEMA copyright ban in Germany: a music copyright agency virtually excluded the entire country from YouTube's pan-European expansion plan. I experienced the disrespectful arrest warrant controversy in Thailand: as YouTube's head of external relations, I faced arrest and was even barred from traveling to the country because of content deemed insulting to the King of Thailand. I witnessed Pakistan cutting off the entire country's internet to ban a single video. I also remember the physical attack on our office in India due to a conflict between global algorithms and local religious taboos. What we truly need to address is never just policy or infrastructure issues, but rather **trust barriers**. In every market, someone must first bear the cost of clarifying which content is safe, acceptable, and valuable before users will participate. This cost accumulates over time, eventually forming a **trust tax**: initially borne by a minority, then shared by everyone. Today, the same contradiction is resurfacing in the field of artificial intelligence, only more severe, evolving more rapidly, and with more noticeable impact. The recent impasse between the US federal government and Anthropic has sparked heated public debate; OpenAI faces increasing scrutiny due to its public sector partnerships. We are witnessing a shift: user acceptance is no longer solely dependent on practicality; ideological influence is deepening. In this environment, trust is extremely fragile; a seemingly minor collapse of trust can trigger a large-scale and rapid loss of users. Google is doubling down on its deep trust strategy, leveraging user familiarity with its existing Workspace and search ecosystems to penetrate the market, but the global landscape is increasingly fragmented. The EU's stringent regulatory red lines, China's fierce AI development race, and escalating AI nationalism have kept the world on high alert. The lessons of 2026 are clear: **Institutional trust and cultural acceptance are now inseparable from the product itself.** Without trust as a foundation, an intelligent operating system cannot be built. This is the sovereign barrier—the structural boundary where global AI clashes with local control. From a product perspective, it manifests more directly as a trust barrier. All expansion of global AI systems will eventually hit this wall. At this critical point, user acceptance no longer depends on technological capabilities, but on whether users, institutions, and governments trust it within their own context. The internet was once borderless. Artificial intelligence will not. The Age of Explorers Ends. The first billion AI users were explorers and technological optimists. But the age of explorers has ended. For the past three years, we've lived in an era of prompt word engineering and digital alchemy. Opening popular apps like ChatGPT and Claude was like entering a digital temple, witnessing the miracles of generative intelligence. In this era, the only important metric was model capability benchmarking: Who topped the latest benchmarks? Who had the largest number of parameters? But as we enter 2026, the bonfire of the age of explorers is dying out. We are no longer building toys for the curious, but turning to intelligent operating systems—those invisible, ubiquitous underlying channels that power the daily operations of individual entrepreneurs in São Paulo, Brazil, and community healthcare workers in Jakarta, Indonesia. These users are not explorers, but rather those with practical needs. They don't want to talk to "ghosts" in machines; they just want a tool that can help them overcome the obstacles in their real lives. This is precisely the moment to truly bridge the chasm in winning over the next billion users. It is also in this unexplored frontier that Silicon Valley's dream of a global API has collided with the harshest reality of our time: **sovereignty barriers**. The core shift lies in the fact that **the popularization of artificial intelligence is no longer primarily a matter of model capabilities, but rather a matter of **dissemination** and **trust**. Cutting-edge labs will continue to improve model performance, but the arrival of the next billion users will not be because a particular model scores higher on benchmark tests, but because artificial intelligence has reached them through institutions, creators, and communities they already trust. The Reality of 2026: AI Becomes a National Infrastructure Issue In 2026, the core challenge for the industry will no longer be making models smarter, but rather granting models **access rights**. Sovereignty barriers represent the boundary where general intelligence meets national identity. Globally, this barrier is already taking shape: data localization requirements, national-level AI computing power initiatives, and government-led model projects in India, the UAE, Europe, and other regions. Initial cloud infrastructure policies are rapidly evolving into intelligent sovereignty policies. Within this framework, nations refuse to become "data colonies," demanding that intelligent systems serving their citizens operate within their own sovereign data warehouses, inheriting local culture and respecting national borders. When you see the CEOs of Google (Sundar Pichai), OpenAI (Sam Altman), Anthropic (Dario Amodei), and DeepMind (Demis Hassabis) appearing on the same stage as Indian Prime Minister Modi at the 2026 India AI Impact Summit, you are witnessing a real-world manifestation of sovereign barriers. Prime Minister Modi's **M.A.N.A.V. Vision** (Ethical System, Accountable Governance, National Sovereignty, AI for All, Trustworthy Systems) sends a clear signal: cutting-edge labs attempting to directly target consumers and establish a foothold will ultimately be eliminated by regulation. And trust is the only currency that transcends these boundaries.

The Dilemma of Weakening Network Effects and Why It Forces New Strategies

Unlike social platforms where each new user increases value for all other users, the value of artificial intelligence is largely localized. The thousandth suggestion I send won't directly make the system more valuable to you. While data flywheels can optimize models, user experience is always personalized, not social. AI is a personal tool; it can have emotional connotations, but its core is a practical tool.

This creates a structural problem: AI cannot rely on the compounding social network effects that propelled the rise of previous-generation platforms. Lacking a native social graph, the industry is trapped in a high-consumption cycle, constantly chasing early adopters, hardcore gamers, and tech elites. This strategy worked in the explorer era, but it cannot scale to reach the next two billion users. More importantly, this model will completely fail in the face of sovereignty barriers. Because when network effects are weak, trust does not form spontaneously but must be introduced externally. Transformation: From Network Effects to Trust Effects If artificial intelligence cannot rely on social network effects to drive its widespread adoption, it must rely on another force: trust networks. This is a crucial shift: From acquiring users to empowering intermediaries. YouTube's ability to scale was due to leveraging existing networks of human trust. AI must do the same. Rather than trying to build direct relationships with billions of users, the winning strategy should be: Empowering those who already have user relationships; Utilizing the trust they've already built; Distributing intelligent capabilities through these channels. Why it's crucial: In a world shaped by sovereign barriers: Distribution channels are limited; Direct-to-user models are fragile; Trust is localized, not globalized. Without strong network effects, artificial intelligence cannot achieve scalability through brute force; it must rely on trust for penetration. Artificial intelligence lacks network effects; it possesses trust effects. Solution: The era of intermediaries is coming. How exactly did YouTube gain a foothold in the international market? It's not about having a better player, nor is it simply about localizing the interface. The key to success is becoming the platform of choice for a local audience with whom they already have a strong sense of trust. In every market, user acceptance doesn't begin with YouTube itself, but with identity anchors—individuals and communities who already hold cultural sway: Bollywood fan pages compile rare Shah Rukh Khan clips for the Dubai expatriate community; American anime enthusiasts build a deep content ecosystem untouched by mainstream media; local comedians, teachers, and mashup creators translate global content into forms that resonate with their local culture. These creators don't just upload videos; they interpret the internet for their audiences, act as intermediaries of trust, and build bridges between overseas platforms and local users. YouTube's success lies in becoming the **invisible infrastructure supporting these identity anchors**. The overlooked core logic: The direct-to-consumer model clashes with sovereign barriers. Most AI companies today still adhere to the direct-to-consumer mindset: **build a better model → present it in a chat interface → directly acquire users**. This model is effective in the short term but difficult to sustain. Because in high-friction markets, users don't directly accept new technologies, but rather through trusted individuals. YouTube's global expansion wasn't about persuading billions of users individually, but about **empowering those who had already earned the audience's trust**. This is the true meaning of invisible infrastructure: **you don't own user relationships, you support them.** At the scalability level, this model possesses a stronger moat.

From Chat to Intelligent Agents: Empowering Trust Intermediaries

This is precisely the key to the shift from chat interfaces to intelligent agents. Chat is a tool geared towards individuals, while intelligent agents provide leverage for intermediaries.

There is a real contradiction here. The vision of artificial intelligence is to achieve general intelligence, but the trend of sovereignty is driving the entire ecosystem toward fragmentation. If each country builds its own technology stack, we will face the risks of incompatible systems, inconsistent security standards, and redundant resource construction. The challenge facing cutting-edge laboratories is not just to increase the scale of intelligence, but to design an architecture that can achieve localized control without weakening the advantages of global collaborative capabilities.

Three Major Structural Shifts in the Intermediary Era

1. AI Distribution Will Enter Existing Trust Networks

AI will not achieve large-scale deployment through independent applications, but will be embedded in instant messaging platforms, creator workflows, education systems, and micro-enterprise infrastructure—because trust has already been established in these scenarios. In the absence of strong network effects, distribution must rely on existing interpersonal networks.

2. National-Level AI Infrastructure Will Become Standard

Governments will increasingly require key AI systems to deploy localized models, build sovereign computing power, or undergo regulatory review, which will accelerate the implementation of sovereign data warehouse architectures.

3. The Creator Economy Will Shift to an Intelligent Agent Economy

Creators will no longer just produce content; they will deploy intelligent agents to perform real tasks for their communities. These intelligent agents will become extensions of trusted individuals, inheriting their reputation and transmitting intelligent capabilities through trust networks.

Of course, there is another possible future: the emergence of a dominant assistant, deeply embedded in operating systems, browsers, and devices, directly establishing a connection between users and models, completely bypassing intermediaries. If this comes to fruition, the trust layer will be directly built into this assistant.

But historical experience points to a more diversified landscape. Even the most dominant platforms—from mobile operating systems to social networks—ultimately rely on ecosystems for growth. Intelligence may be universal, but trust is always localized. Regardless of which architecture ultimately prevails, the core challenge will not change: the popularization of AI is no longer primarily a model issue, but a distribution and trust issue.

Conclusion: Niche Markets are the True Global Markets. The biggest fallacy of the Explorer Era was believing that intelligence was a standardized commodity—a single global API that behaved exactly the same in a Manhattan conference room and a rural Karnataka village. Sovereignty barriers reveal a harsher truth: intelligence may be universal, but widespread adoption is not. National and local institutions don't want a black-box external system; they want control, adaptability, and the right to shape intelligence within their own boundaries. They don't want ready-made applications, but the underlying infrastructure—infrastructure, security systems, and computing power—that their citizens can build independently. The growth logic in 2026 will no longer be about finding a universal user experience, but about product flexibility—allowing intelligence to adapt to local scenarios, regulations, and cultures without sacrificing core capabilities. If we continue to directly pursue global consumers, we will always remain an external layer—vulnerable, substitutable, and will repeat the shocks I experienced at YouTube. But when we shift towards empowering intermediaries, the model will be completely transformed: from chat interfaces to intelligent agents, from persuading users to empowering trust-based intermediaries, from resisting regulation to turning regulation into a moat. The scaling of artificial intelligence relies not on models, but on trust. The winners of the AI ​​race will not be the companies with the smartest models, but those that can best empower local heroes—teachers, accountants, community leaders—tenfold. Because ultimately, intelligence is transmitted within systems, but its dissemination occurs among people.

data.title: This company uses AI to create all its content, and its readers have all left.
data.description:
This isn't that AI is ineffective. This is that relying entirely on AI for content creation has reached a dead end.

In March 2026, BuzzFeed released its full-year 2025 financial report.

The company suffered a net loss of $57.3 million for the year, nearly 70% higher than in 2024. The cumulative loss has reached $680 million.

In March 2026, BuzzFeed released its full-year 2025 financial report.

2023, All in AI

In early 2023, BuzzFeed CEO Jonah Peretti sent an internal letter to all employees, the core message of which was: We must fully embrace AI.

The news made Wall Street happy. The stock price surged more than 120% that day—the story investors love to hear, "AI + layoffs = profits."

How long did the rise last? Less than a month later, it fell back down.

But Peretti didn't stop. In April 2023, BuzzFeed laid off 15% of its staff and shut down its BuzzFeed News division. What was this division like? It had just won the Pulitzer Prize in 2021 and was a team that had done in-depth investigative reporting. It was shut down. A month later, Peretti added publicly, "AI will replace most of the static content on websites." In short: editors and reporters are too expensive, AI is cheap, so switch. At the time, many thought this was a natural move, an "embracing of technological change." After all, ChatGPT had just become popular, and everyone wanted to jump on the bandwagon. But looking back now, this wasn't embracing change; it was selling its soul.

Vicious Cycle

What happened at BuzzFeed after they cut their editorial team?

First step: Content deteriorated.

Previously, it was written by humans. Editors would select topics, verify facts, and add their own judgment and tone to the articles. Now it's all AI-generated—topics are templated, the writing is bland and predictable, and it reads like a fill-in-the-blank exercise.

A popular comment on Reddit bluntly stated, "3 years after switching to AI word slop, BuzzFeed is going out of business." This comment garnered over 15,000 likes. The second step: readers left. The term "word slop" is particularly apt—it describes that feeling of something being off, but you can't quite put your finger on it. Readers aren't stupid; they can tell what's genuinely written by a human and what's just machine-generated content. BuzzFeed's traffic began to decline steadily. The third step: revenue collapsed. The decline in traffic directly impacted advertising revenue. Advertising revenue in 2025 was nearly $3 million less than in 2024. Not much, but enough to make things even harder for an already loss-making company. The fourth step: even less money to hire staff. Declining revenue means even less money to hire editors to improve content quality, creating a vicious cycle—the more money saved, the worse the content; the worse the content, the fewer readers leave; the fewer readers leave, the less money is made. A reader left a comment on Reddit that I think perfectly encapsulates this cycle: "The readers know there's no one home." 2026, the numbers don't lie. [Image of a PNG file] So, what are the real statistics? In 2025, the company reported a net loss of $57.3 million, a 68% increase from $34 million in 2024. Its stock price has fallen 77%, from $3 three years ago to $0.70 now. The accumulated losses have reached $680 million. Most intriguingly, BuzzFeed itself is hesitant to provide a 2026 earnings forecast. The financial report essentially states, "We don't know if we'll survive next year." This isn't just a single misstep; it's the collapse of an entire narrative of "replacing humans with AI." AI is not the soul. AI can help you write faster, but it cannot replace the person behind the screen. BuzzFeed's mistake wasn't using AI—it used AI to eliminate that person. The correct approach is never "AI replacing humans," but rather "AI enhancing humans." Humans make the judgments, AI performs the execution. Humans write the soul, AI writes the length. What does this mean for us? BuzzFeed has fallen, but the lessons it learned are worth remembering. First, content is more than just "information." Information can be quantified, but trust cannot. Readers aren't buying two thousand words; they're buying "the person who wrote this article is worth my five minutes." Second, AI is leverage, not a substitute. Using AI to help you research, polish your work, and create headlines is leverage. But letting AI make topic selection decisions, express opinions, and build relationships with readers for you is suicidal. Third, the cost of "efficiency" may be underestimated. Peretti thought he could save a lot of money by laying off 15% of his staff, but he didn't consider the losses in readership, trust, and brand value. Short-term financial statements look good, but the long-term foundation is rotten. BuzzFeed may struggle for a while longer; its financial report says it's "exploring strategic options," and it doesn't rule out being sold or acquired. But it taught everyone a lesson: You can use AI to do anything, but don't use AI to replace your value as a human being. Readers aren't there to read content; they're there to "feel another person." Without that person, even the best content is just an empty shell.
